Test coverage: no v2 test exercises the 100–255 character range. All `description=` literals in tests/spikesorting/v2/ are short strings (<50 chars). But the boundary is a MySQL DDL property, not application logic — meaningful unit-test coverage is not really expected here.

Classification: the change is intentional (it appears in the design plan) and the rationale, while not spelled out, is obvious from codebase convention. Calling it "intentional_unjustified" is defensible but borderline — there is partial justification by convention even if not stated for this specific column. Severity is low; this is a strict-widening DDL change with no functional regression risk.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 master schema adds metrics_source enum column (no v1 equivalent)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 schema (v1/curation.py:29-41) has columns: sorting_id, curation_id, parent_curation_id, analysis_file_name, object_id, merges_applied, description. No metrics-provenance column.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 schema (v2/curation.py:54-64) adds `metrics_source = 'manual': enum('manual', 'analyzer_curation', 'figpack')` between merges_applied and description.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Column visible in schema. Only the 'manual' value is exercised in tests (test_single_session_pipeline.py:1847). 'analyzer_curation' and 'figpack' have no test coverage exercising the value-side path — the enum validator (line 304-313) is implicitly tested only via MetricsSource.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Module docstring (v2/curation.py:6-9) explains: 'metrics_source is restricted to true CurationV2 provenance values; external or ground-truth NWB Units continue to use ImportedSpikeSorting'. The two non-default values reserve space for future MetricCuration / figpack workflows.

verdict.reasoning:
All claims verified by direct read:

v1 schema at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:29-41 has:
```
-> SpikeSorting
curation_id=0: int
---
parent_curation_id=-1: int
-> AnalysisNwbfile
object_id: varchar(72)
merges_applied: bool
description: varchar(100)
```
No metrics_source column.

v2 schema at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:54-64 has the same columns plus a new line 62: `metrics_source = 'manual': enum('manual', 'analyzer_curation', 'figpack')` positioned between merges_applied and description. (Also note: v2 widens description to varchar(255) and defaults merges_applied=0 — but that wasn't the claim.)

The module docstring at v2/curation.py:6-9 documents the rationale exactly as quoted: "metrics_source is restricted to true CurationV2 provenance values (manual, analyzer_curation, figpack). External or ground-truth NWB Units continue to use ImportedSpikeSorting; v2 does NOT duplicate them into CurationV2."

The enum validator at v2/curation.py:304-313 coerces metrics_source through MetricsSource(...) so a typo raises ValueError up-front instead of producing a MySQL enum-mismatch error.

MetricsSource enum defined at v2/utils.py:36-47 with three values matching the schema: manual, analyzer_curation, figpack.

Test coverage: only one occurrence in v2 tests — tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:1847 — `assert row["metrics_source"] == "manual"`. The non-default enum values ('analyzer_curation', 'figpack') have no test exercising the value-side path. The reviewer's claim that they're "implicitly tested only via MetricsSource" is accurate — the validator's ValueError branch (lines 308-313) is reachable from any insert with an invalid string, but no test sends an invalid string. Severity remains low: forward-compatible additive enum with safe default and a fail-fast validator, justified by documented future-work plan (figpack_curation.py and metric_curation.py are known stubs per repo context).

confirmed_findings.title: v2 get_sorting(as_dataframe=True) returns 2-column DataFrame; v1 returns the full NWB units DataFrame
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 (v1/curation.py:197-209) opens the units NWB and returns nwbf.units.to_dataframe() directly — carries spike_times, curation_label, merge_groups, AND every per-metric column added at insert time (e.g. snr, isi_violation).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 (v2/curation.py:990-1026) builds a custom DataFrame with only spike_times and curation_label (joined from CurationV2.UnitLabel). No merge_groups column (it is in DataJoint, not the NWB), no metrics columns (none stored), no other NWB unit columns.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Comment at v2/curation.py:994-1000 explains the curation_label join rationale. Does not call out that downstream consumers checking df['merge_groups'] or any metric column will see KeyError on v2. Test test_get_sorting_dataframe_includes_curation_label (line 5878+) covers presence of curation_label.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Partially documented. The metric-columns gap follows from the missing-MetricCuration gap; the merge_groups gap is documented in the MergeGroup part-table docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims directly against source.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:197-209):
```
analysis_file_name = (CurationV1 & key).fetch1("analysis_file_name")
...
with pynwb.NWBHDF5IO(analysis_file_abs_path, "r", load_namespaces=True) as io:
    nwbf = io.read()
    units = nwbf.units.to_dataframe()
if as_dataframe:
    return units
```
v1 returns the full NWB units DataFrame. Confirmed at v1/curation.py:390-428 that v1 writes `curation_label` (index=True), `merge_groups` (index=True), AND one column per metric (e.g. snr, isi_violation) to the NWB units table at insert_curation time. So v1's as_dataframe path carries spike_times + curation_label + merge_groups + per-metric columns.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:989-1026):
```
if not as_dataframe:
    return si_sorting
...
return pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "spike_times": spike_times,
        "curation_label": [labels_by_unit.get(uid, []) for uid in unit_ids],
    },
    index=pd.Index(unit_ids, name="unit_id"),
)
```
v2 builds a fresh 2-column DataFrame (spike_times + curation_label joined from CurationV2.UnitLabel). No merge_groups, no metric columns. Confirmed at v2/curation.py:864-876 that v2 only writes `curation_label` to the NWB units table (not merge_groups, not metrics), so the NWB itself doesn't even carry those columns — they live in DataJoint part tables instead.

Rationale documented at v2/curation.py:994-999 explains the curation_label join (mirrors v1 shape so external notebooks reading df['curation_label'] still work). Merge_groups absence is consistent with v2's MergeGroup part-table design. Metrics absence follows from MetricCuration being a stub (per environment notes).

Test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:5878-5911 (`test_get_sorting_dataframe_includes_curation_label`) pins `curation_label` presence/values only. No test asserts what happens when downstream code calls df['merge_groups'] or df['snr'] — these would have worked on v1 and now KeyError on v2. The backwards-incompat shape change is real but only partially documented (curation_label join is explained inline; merge_groups/metrics absence is implicit from the omitted-columns design, not called out as a breaking change to downstream consumers).

Classification: intentional_justified is correct — v2's design intentionally moves merge_groups to a part table (recent commits explicitly work on the MergeGroup design) and treats MetricCuration as a known stub. The redesign is documented in the code architecture, though the downstream-impact note (df['merge_groups']/df['<metric>'] KeyError) is not explicit.

Severity medium is correct — API shape change that downstream notebook code can hit, but not a silent numerical regression.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 dispatches sample-frame conversion through NwbSortingExtractor; v1 uses bespoke spike_times_to_valid_samples
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 get_sorting (v1/curation.py:213-225) calls spike_times_to_valid_samples(recording_times, unit.spike_times, n_samples, unit.Index) per-unit, which guards a documented searchsorted boundary bug at the last sample (v1/sorting.py:39-50): a spike time exactly equal to recording_times[-1] would otherwise map to index n_samples (out of bounds).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 get_sorting (v2/curation.py:986-988) constructs NwbSortingExtractor(file_path=abs_path, sampling_frequency=fs, t_start=t_start) which uses SI's internal sample conversion via t_start + sample/fs round-trip. The boundary-spike behavior depends on SI's NwbSortingExtractor implementation, not on spike_times_to_valid_samples.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at line 958-964 explicitly says 'CurationV2's units NWB does not carry an ElectricalSeries, so we must supply sampling_frequency and t_start directly (mirrors Sorting.get_sorting). t_start is the recording's first wall-clock timestamp.' Test test_single_session_pipeline.py:3712+ (boundary-spike round-trip) covers the SI-side equivalent. v1's hand-rolled boundary fix is no longer enforced in v2 — only SI's behavior matters.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2 moves to SortingAnalyzer API per the stated goal; documented in the get_sorting docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
All cited code lines verified.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:211-225) calls per-unit:
  units_dict = {unit.Index: spike_times_to_valid_samples(recording_times, unit.spike_times, n_samples, unit.Index) for unit in units.itertuples()}
  return si.NumpySorting.from_unit_dict([units_dict], sampling_frequency=sampling_frequency)

The helper at v1/sorting.py:29-79 documents the boundary bug guard. Its docstring says: "floating-point rounding in the seconds-to-samples round-trip can cause np.searchsorted to return an index equal to n_samples ... SpikeInterface rejects such a sorting with ValueError: 'The sorting object has spikes exceeding the recording duration'. This helper drops those out-of-bounds indices and emits a warning."

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:982-988) builds the sorting via:
  si_sorting = NwbSortingExtractor(file_path=abs_path, sampling_frequency=fs, t_start=t_start)
with explicit zero-unit guard above it.

v2 docstring at lines 958-961 documents the rationale: "CurationV2's units NWB does not carry an ElectricalSeries, so we must supply sampling_frequency and t_start directly (mirrors Sorting.get_sorting). t_start is the recording's first wall-clock timestamp."

The reviewer's test reference is essentially correct: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:3682+ (test_boundary_spike_round_trip_does_not_raise, line 3686) explicitly gates this decision. Its docstring states: "This test gates the decision on whether to fold in v1's spike_times_to_valid_samples clip ... If SI 0.104 raises here, the v1 clip is required on read; if it does not raise, the clip is unnecessary and the test is kept as a regression guard." It tests both Sorting.get_sorting (line 3814) and CurationV2.get_sorting (line 3831) read paths with a hand-planted boundary spike at n_samples - 1.

Classification holds: this is intentional_justified. v2 author moved to SI's NwbSortingExtractor (SortingAnalyzer-aligned), documented the rationale in-code, and added a regression-guard test that explicitly evaluates whether the v1-style guard remains necessary on SI 0.104. Severity medium is appropriate: if SI ever regresses on boundary handling, both read paths would break and the bug would surface, but the test guards against it.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 get_sort_group_info returns ALL electrodes; v1 returns only one electrode per sort_group via fetch(limit=1)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 get_sort_group_info (v1/curation.py:288-302) iterates `for entry in table:` and does `electrode_restrict_list.extend(((SortGroup.SortGroupElectrode() & entry) * Electrode).fetch(limit=1))` — only the FIRST electrode per sort group is included, so a multi-region probe surfaces only one region per group.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 get_sort_group_info (v2/curation.py:1193-1236) returns (SortGroupV2.SortGroupElectrode & sg_restriction) * _Electrode * BrainRegion — every electrode in the sort group joined to its BrainRegion. Returns a DataJoint relation, not a DataFrame.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at line 1196-1200 explicitly calls out: 'Fix for the v1 fetch(limit=1) multi-region under-reporting bug: this returns a DataJoint relation (not a DataFrame, not single-row) covering EVERY electrode in the sort group so a multi-region probe surfaces every represented region.' The return-type change (Table vs DataFrame) is a callsite-breaking API divergence.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented in v2 docstring as an explicit bugfix.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly at the cited lines.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:282-302) builds `electrode_restrict_list` by iterating `for entry in table` and calling `((SortGroup.SortGroupElectrode() & entry) * Electrode).fetch(limit=1)` — pulling only the FIRST electrode per sort group. The final return `(cls & key).proj() * sort_group_info` is then effectively single-row-per-sort-group.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:1193-1236) returns `(SortGroupV2.SortGroupElectrode & sg_restriction) * _Electrode * BrainRegion` — every electrode in the sort group, joined to BrainRegion. No `limit=1` anywhere.

The v2 docstring at lines 1195-1208 explicitly documents this as a fix for the v1 `fetch(limit=1)` multi-region under-reporting bug, and additionally explains the `@classmethod` promotion to keep the merge-table dispatcher at `spikesorting_merge.py:412` (confirmed via grep) from raising TypeError.

One minor inaccuracy in the reviewer summary: v1 also returns a `dj.Table` (declared as `-> dj.Table` at v1/curation.py:269), not a DataFrame. So the "DataFrame vs DataJoint relation" framing in the reviewer's claim is slightly off — both return DataJoint relations. The substantive divergence is single-row-per-group vs all-electrodes, plus instance vs classmethod. The v2 docstring's "(not a DataFrame, not single-row)" parenthetical is contrasting against callsite assumptions (callers may have been doing `.fetch1()` expecting one row), not against v1's literal return type.

Test coverage is solid:
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:1962 (`test_curation_v2_get_sort_group_info_returns_all_electrodes`)
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:5663 (merge dispatch test)
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_downstream_consumers.py:189
- tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:519 (listed in compared API set)

Classification `intentional_justified` is correct — documented as a bug fix with rationale and covered by tests. Severity `high` is appropriate: this changes the contract callers see (single row → multi-row), and any v1 callers using `.fetch1()` on the result will break on v2, but it fixes a silent multi-region data-loss bug.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 lazy-merge id assignment can diverge from applied-merge ids when user input order differs from kept-uid-ascending order
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 only ever applies merges synchronously at insert; the v1 lazy path via get_merged_sorting reads merge_groups from NWB and reconstructs via _merge_dict_to_list which already loses input order. v1's applied-merge ids follow user-provided order via `for merge_group in merge_groups: new_unit_id = max(...) + 1` (v1/curation.py:359-367).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 applied path (apply_merge=True) assigns fresh merged ids in USER-PROVIDED group order (v2/curation.py:599-602, 652-658). v2 lazy path (get_merged_sorting on apply_merge=False) reads MergeGroup rows ordered by (unit_id, contributor_unit_id) (v2/curation.py:1051-1056), so the lazy path iterates by kept-uid-ascending. When user-given group order differs from kept-uid order, applied and lazy paths assign the same fresh ids to different content groups.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at line 175-179 calls this out: 'NOTE: the lazy merge path reads MergeGroup ordered by unit_id and so iterates by kept-uid-ascending; when input order != kept-uid order, applied and lazy paths assign the same fresh ids to different content groups.' Duplicated NOTE at line 590-598. Grep of tests shows no test exercises this input-order-vs-kept-uid-order disagreement; the apply_merge=True+False parity test (test_single_session_pipeline.py:4552-4794) uses head=units[0], absorbed=units[1] (already ascending), so it does not hit the divergence path.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Acknowledged in docstring as a known divergence between v2's own two paths (applied vs lazy). v1 parity is preserved only for the applied path.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all four cited locations:

1. v1 applied path at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:359-367 iterates merge groups in user-given order and assigns `np.max(list(units_dict.keys())) + 1` per group — confirmed.

2. v2 applied path at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:599-602, 650-658: `normalized_groups` preserves user order; `next_merged_id = max(by_id) + 1` and `for int_group in normalized_groups: if apply_merge and len(int_group) > 1: key = next_merged_id; next_merged_id += 1` — confirmed user-order assignment.

3. v2 lazy path at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:1051-1056: `order_by=("unit_id", "contributor_unit_id")` orders rows by ascending `unit_id` (kept-uid), and `get_merged_sorting` at 1090-1109 iterates this dict's values to build `units_to_merge` for SI's `MergeUnitsSorting`. SI assigns ids in iteration order. Confirmed kept-uid-ascending iteration.

4. Docstring NOTE at 174-179 explicitly acknowledges: "the lazy merge path reads MergeGroup ordered by unit_id and so iterates by kept-uid-ascending; when input order != kept-uid order, applied and lazy paths assign the same fresh ids to different content groups." Duplicated in code comment at 587-598.

5. Test coverage: `test_curation_two_merge_groups_assign_ids_in_user_input_order` at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:4853-4921 DOES test the applied path with non-ascending input `[[2, 3], [0, 1]]` and verifies new ids 5 and 6 follow user order. It also tests that apply_merge=False preview rows are deterministic. BUT it does NOT invoke `get_merged_sorting` on the preview to compare the lazy-path assigned ids against the applied-path assigned ids for the same non-ascending input. The only `get_merged_sorting` test call (line 4784) uses an ascending merge group, so it cannot reveal the documented divergence.

The reviewer's claim is correct: the cross-path id-assignment divergence is acknowledged in the docstring as a known tradeoff (v1 parity for the applied path is the explicit goal), but no test exercises the specific case where user input order differs from kept-uid ascending order AND both paths' assigned ids are compared. Classification "untested_branch" is appropriate — the divergence is documented but the cross-path inconsistency branch lacks direct test coverage. Severity "medium" is reasonable: the divergence is intentional/documented for the applied path's v1 parity, but the silent content-to-id mismatch between v2's own two paths could confuse downstream consumers who switch between apply_merge modes.

confirmed_findings.title: v1 _consolidate_intervals has an end-frame off-by-one + a sort bug; v2 fixes both but documents only the first
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: stop_indices uses `np.searchsorted(..., side="right") - 1`, producing an INCLUSIVE stop index that is then passed to SI's frame_slice whose end_frame is EXCLUSIVE -- silently drops the last sample of every disjoint interval (~33 us @ 30 kHz). Additionally, the unsorted-fallback path runs `intervals = np.sort(intervals, axis=0)` which sorts EACH COLUMN INDEPENDENTLY, scrambling the (start, stop) pairing.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Uses `np.searchsorted(..., side="right")` directly as the exclusive end (matches SI convention) AND uses `intervals[np.argsort(intervals[:, 0])]` (stable order by start), preserving pair integrity. Adjacency condition becomes `next_start <= stop` (vs v1's `stop >= next_start - 1`).
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at lines 442-450 explicitly calls out the v1 off-by-one and explains the new semantics. The columnwise-sort fix is silent: v2 just writes `intervals[np.argsort(intervals[:, 0])]` at line 478 without naming v1's `np.sort(..., axis=0)` as a separate bug. Both bugs are real: v1 line 735 `np.sort(intervals, axis=0)` actively scrambles pairs and is hit whenever the input is not already sorted.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2 docstring (utils.py:442-450) documents the off-by-one fix; the columnwise sort fix is undocumented but clearly intentional.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims directly against source.

v1 off-by-one (recording.py:742-744):
```
stop_indices = (
    np.searchsorted(timestamps, intervals[:, 1], side="right") - 1
)
```
This produces an INCLUSIVE end index, but it is passed at recording.py:569 as `end_frame=interval_indices[1]` to SI's `frame_slice`, whose end_frame is EXCLUSIVE. Confirmed: drops the last sample of every disjoint interval.

v1 columnwise sort bug (recording.py:735):
```
intervals = np.sort(intervals, axis=0)
```
`np.sort(..., axis=0)` sorts each column independently, scrambling (start, stop) pairs. Confirmed; only hit on the unsorted-input fallback path.

v2 fixes (utils.py):
- Line 478: `intervals = _np.argsort(intervals[:, 0])` based reindex preserves pair integrity.
- Lines 486-488: uses `side="right"` directly, NO `- 1`, as exclusive end matching SI's frame_slice convention.
- Line 499: adjacency condition is `if next_start <= stop:` — the correct exclusive-end form (commented at line 495-498 as equivalent under the off-by-one rewrite).

Documentation:
- utils.py:438-450 docstring explicitly calls out the v1 off-by-one and explains semantics. CONFIRMED documented.
- The columnwise-sort fix is NOT explicitly named as a v1 bug in the docstring. Line 476 comment ("v1 did the same. Stable ordering by start") is misleading: v1 did NOT do stable-by-start ordering. So the sort-bug fix is silent / partially mis-documented.

Test coverage:
- grep finds `_consolidate_intervals` referenced only in `test_disjoint_sort_intervals_concatenated` (test_single_session_pipeline.py:5705-5840). That test exercises a SORTED 2-chunk disjoint case at the Recording.make level. It does NOT directly unit-test `_consolidate_intervals`, does NOT cover the unsorted-input fallback (the path containing the v1 columnwise-sort bug fix), and uses a 5% length slack so it does not specifically pin the off-by-one. The unsorted-fallback branch in v2 (line 477-478) is effectively untested.

Severity: high — silent loss of one sample per disjoint interval, and silent pair-scrambling on unsorted inputs in v1, both fixed in v2. Classification `intentional_justified` holds for the off-by-one (documented); the columnwise-sort fix is intentional but under-documented. Overall, the divergence is real and the v2 behavior is the correct one.

Caveats noted: docstring claim "v1 did the same" at utils.py:476 is technically inaccurate w.r.t. column-independent sorting.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 drops v1's per-group reference-electrode handling in shank grouping
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: `get_group_by_shank` (utils.py:13-138) supports two reference-resolution paths: (a) caller-supplied `references` dict mapping each electrode_group to its ref electrode id; (b) when None, reads `original_reference_electrode` from each Electrode row and asserts all shank electrodes share the same ref. Result: each sort group can have its own `sort_reference_electrode_id`.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: `SortGroupV2.set_group_by_shank` only accepts a single scalar `sort_reference_electrode_id: int = -1` applied UNIFORMLY to every sort group. The docstring states `Per-group references are not supported in v2; if you need them, build the rows manually.` No code reads `original_reference_electrode`.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/recording.py:262-263 explicitly documents the limitation but gives no rationale (no design note, no commit message explaining why per-group refs were dropped). Labs whose Electrode tables encode per-tetrode references via `original_reference_electrode` lose that automation; they must manually build rows.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: no documented rationale

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the divergence holds.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:13-138, called from src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:88-93):
- `get_group_by_shank` accepts `references: dict = None` keyed by electrode_group.
- If `references` provided, looks up per-group ref (utils.py:76-82).
- If None, reads `original_reference_electrode` from Electrode rows (utils.py:83-93) and asserts all shank electrodes share the same ref. Result: each sort group can carry its own `sort_reference_electrode_id` (utils.py:94).
- v1 SortGroup.set_group_by_shank signature includes `references: dict = None` (v1/recording.py:54).

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:232-273):
- Signature accepts only `sort_reference_electrode_id: int = -1` (line 238). No `references` param.
- The same scalar is applied uniformly to every sort group's master row (line 379): `"sort_reference_electrode_id": sort_reference_electrode_id`.
- Docstring lines 262-263 explicitly: "Per-group references are not supported in v2; if you need them, build the rows manually."
- `set_group_by_electrode_table_column` (line 421) shows the same single-scalar pattern.
- `grep original_reference_electrode src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/` returns no hits — the NWB-derived per-group fallback is gone entirely.

Rationale: I found no design note or commit message justifying dropping per-group refs; the only documentation is the docstring acknowledging the limitation. Git log shows no commits explaining the choice.

Test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py exercises `set_group_by_shank` extensively but always with default `sort_reference_electrode_id=-1` (no per-group reference behavior probed). test_single_session_pipeline.py:5224 comment even notes "Every other recording test uses sort_reference_electrode_id =..." (uniform).

Classification: intentional_unjustified is correct — the docstring proves intent; absence of rationale plus no migration note/escape hatch beyond "build rows manually" makes it unjustified.

Severity: medium is defensible. Labs whose Electrode tables encode per-tetrode references via `original_reference_electrode` lose the automation entirely; they must hand-build master rows. Not a correctness bug because manual workaround exists and reference handling in the recording pipeline still functions; it's a feature/usability regression. I would lean low-to-medium; sticking with medium since loss of NWB-derived automation could silently change which reference is used on rerun for sites that depended on it.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 reimplements artifact detection in-memory, abandoning v1's ChunkRecordingExecutor + worker helpers
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 ships `_init_artifact_worker` (utils.py:141-158) and `_compute_artifact_chunk` (utils.py:161-205) for SI's ChunkRecordingExecutor parallel scan; combined-threshold mode uses `np.logical_or(above_z, above_a)`.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 inlines the entire scan in `ArtifactDetection._detect_artifacts` (artifact.py:828-925) using full `recording.get_traces()` in memory, no chunking, no parallel workers. Comment at v2/artifact.py:866-868 acknowledges 'In-memory scan: acceptable for recordings up to a few minutes; ... Chunked iteration is follow-up work alongside the recompute pipeline.'
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 comment at artifact.py:866-868 admits the in-memory approach is a stopgap. Logic is otherwise equivalent (OR-combine, axis=1 z-score, proportion threshold). But for long-recording sessions (>>few minutes) v2 will run out of memory where v1 streamed chunks. No memory-budget check, no fallback. Tests don't appear to exercise long recordings.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: v2 author acknowledges it as 'follow-up work' in the comment; no further justification given.

verdict.reasoning:
All claims verified directly against the code.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:141-205): `_init_artifact_worker` and `_compute_artifact_chunk` are SI ChunkRecordingExecutor worker functions. Combined-threshold mode uses `np.logical_or(above_z, above_a)` at line 198. v1/artifact.py:278-307 wires these into ChunkRecordingExecutor with `n_jobs` from `job_kwargs`, supporting parallel chunked scans.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/artifact.py:828-925): `_detect_artifacts` is a single staticmethod. Line 870: `traces = recording.get_traces(return_in_uV=False)` loads the ENTIRE recording into memory in one call. No chunk loop, no n_jobs, no ChunkRecordingExecutor. Logic is otherwise equivalent: axis=1 z-score (lines 901-903), amplitude threshold (line 883), OR-combine (line 919), proportion-above check (line 925).

Stopgap acknowledgement verified verbatim at v2/artifact.py:866-869: "In-memory scan: acceptable for recordings up to a few minutes; the smoke / 60s polymer fixtures fit easily. Chunked iteration is follow-up work alongside the recompute pipeline."

Test coverage of `_detect_artifacts` exists (tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:3841, 3952, 4129, 4196) but all use synthetic / 60s polymer fixtures. No test exercises long-recording memory behavior; no test asserts a memory budget or chunked fallback.

Classification "intentional_unjustified" stands: the author explicitly knows about the limitation and labels it "follow-up work" without adding a guard (no recording-length check, no memory-budget warning, no chunked fallback). Severity "high" stands because a typical chronic Frank-lab recording (30kHz × tens of channels × hours) will OOM silently rather than producing a clear schema-level rejection. This is a real regression risk for any non-fixture session even though it doesn't affect correctness of the algorithm itself.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 _check_artifact_thresholds equivalent is absent; validation lives in Pydantic schema
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has dedicated `_check_artifact_thresholds` helper (utils.py:208-257) that (a) raises ValueError for negative thresholds and (b) WARNS and clamps `proportion_above_thresh` if out of [0, 1] (clamps <0 to 0.01 with warning, >1 to 1 with warning) -- a forgiving runtime check.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 has no equivalent helper. Validation happens implicitly via `ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema` (Pydantic) at insert time. If schema constraints (which I did not re-verify here) reject out-of-range values, the v1 warn-and-clamp behavior is lost -- callers get a hard error instead of a clamp.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2's general schema-first design strategy (per commit history and module docstring at v2/utils.py:225-267 _assert_schema_version_matches) intentionally moves validation upstream. Behavioral difference: v1's `proportion_above_thresh < 0 -> warn + clamp to 0.01` becomes a hard rejection. Documented design preference for strict validation makes the change justified, but the specific clamp-vs-reject decision is not called out.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Implied by v2's overall schema-first design; not explicitly justified for this case.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the claim directly against code.

v1 helper exists at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:208-257 with exact described behavior:
- Lines 236-240: raises ValueError if amplitude/zscore thresholds are negative
- Lines 243-249: WARNS and clamps proportion_above_thresh < 0 to 0.01
- Lines 250-256: WARNS and clamps proportion_above_thresh > 1 to 1

v1 call site confirmed at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/artifact.py:267-275.

v2 has no _check_artifact_thresholds helper (grep on v2/artifact.py confirms). Validation lives in /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:
- Line 54: `amplitude_thresh_uV: float | None = Field(default=500.0, ge=0.0)` — hard reject negative (matches v1 ValueError)
- Line 55-67: `zscore_thresh: ... ge=0.0` — hard reject negative
- Line 68: `proportion_above_thresh: float = Field(default=1.0, gt=0.0, le=1.0)` — hard reject out-of-range AND zero (v1 only rejected <0)

Behavioral divergence is real:
1. v1 `proportion_above_thresh < 0`: warn + clamp to 0.01 → v2: ValidationError
2. v1 `proportion_above_thresh > 1`: warn + clamp to 1 → v2: ValidationError
3. v1 `proportion_above_thresh == 0`: silently allowed → v2: ValidationError (gt=0.0)

Behavior is tested in v2: /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/tests/spikesorting/v2/test_params_validation.py:111-116 explicitly asserts that 0.0 and 1.5 raise ValidationError.

Classification: intentional_justified is correct. v2's schema-first design is documented (module docstring on v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:8-34) and Pydantic Field constraints make the strict bounds explicit. Tests guard the new behavior. The specific clamp→reject tradeoff isn't called out in the schema docstring, but it follows from the project-wide schema-first policy.

Severity: low is correct — this is parameter-validation surface only. Strict rejection is arguably safer than silent clamping (which could hide user typos). The change only affects users who pass invalid params; valid configurations are unaffected.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 _get_recording_timestamps adds optional override parameter and changes dtype handling
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: `_get_recording_timestamps(recording)` -- single arg; uses `np.zeros((total_frames,))` (default float64 already) and direct integer indexing from numpy cumsum (no int() casts).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: `_get_recording_timestamps(recording, override=None)` -- adds `override` kwarg that short-circuits to caller-supplied repaired-timestamp array. Forces `dtype=_np.float64` explicitly and `int()`-casts the index bounds.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring (utils.py:381-394) explicitly explains the `override` is the hook for the non-monotonic-timestamps repair that's in-flight on v1's `copilot/fix-populating-artifact-detection` branch. Recording.make passes corrected timestamps through; out-of-make callers leave override=None and get the same behavior as v1.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented in v2/utils.py:386-394 docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at the cited locations.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:260-279):
```
def _get_recording_timestamps(recording):
    ...
    cumsum_frames = np.cumsum(frames_per_segment)
    total_frames = np.sum(frames_per_segment)
    timestamps = np.zeros((total_frames,))
    for i in range(num_segments):
        start_index = cumsum_frames[i]
        end_index = cumsum_frames[i + 1]
        timestamps[start_index:end_index] = recording.get_times(segment_index=i)
    return timestamps
```
Single arg, default-dtype zeros, no int() casts.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/utils.py:366-432):
```
def _get_recording_timestamps(recording, override=None):
    ...
    if override is not None:
        return _np.asarray(override)
    ...
    total_frames = int(cumsum_frames[-1])
    timestamps = _np.zeros((total_frames,), dtype=_np.float64)
    for i in range(num_segments):
        start_index = int(cumsum_frames[i])
        end_index = int(cumsum_frames[i + 1])
        timestamps[start_index:end_index] = recording.get_times(segment_index=i)
    return timestamps
```
Adds `override` kwarg, explicit float64, int() casts on cumsum indices.

Docstring at v2/utils.py:381-394 explicitly documents the `override` parameter as the threading hook for non-monotonic-timestamp repair from Recording.make, and notes it mirrors v1's helper with the additional plumbing.

Verified callers in v2/recording.py:1298-1302, 1411, 1595, 1879: Recording.make passes `override=timestamps_override` on single-interval writes, while non-make/multi-interval paths call without override (preserving v1 behavior).

Verified test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:1589-1609 asserts the override is non-None on single-interval writes ("single-interval write got timestamps_override=None; ..."), exercising the override threading. The int() casts and explicit dtype are defensive no-ops (np.cumsum of int lists already yields int64, and float64 is numpy's default), with no behavioral impact for the override=None code path.

Backward-compatible when override=None; documented justification; out-of-make callers get v1-equivalent behavior; test-covered. Severity low — this is plumbing for a future repair feature, not a behavior change on default paths.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 omits _reformat_metrics helper used by v1 FigURLCuration
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's shared utils provides `_reformat_metrics` (utils.py:282-294) which reshapes a Dict[metric_name, Dict[unit_id, value]] into the sortingview-expected list-of-dicts format with name/label/tooltip/data fields. Used by `v1/figurl_curation.py:194`.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 has no equivalent helper. v2/figpack_curation.py is a 3-line stub (per the known-gaps list), so the absent helper is consistent with the absent feature.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 figpack_curation is a known stub; the missing helper will need porting when the figpack feature is implemented.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: FigURLCuration is a known-gap feature; helper porting is deferred with the feature.

verdict.reasoning:
Directly verified all claims:

1. v1 `_reformat_metrics` exists at src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:282-294 exactly as described:
```python
def _reformat_metrics(metrics: Dict[str, Dict[str, float]]) -> List[Dict]:
    return [
        {
            "name": metric_name,
            "label": metric_name,
            "tooltip": metric_name,
            "data": {
                str(unit_id): metric_value
                for unit_id, metric_value in metric.items()
            },
        }
        for metric_name, metric in metrics.items()
    ]
```
Signature, fields (name/label/tooltip/data), and shape transformation all match the reviewer's description.

2. v1/figurl_curation.py imports it (line 12: `from spyglass.spikesorting.utils import _reformat_metrics`) and uses it at line 194 (`unit_metrics = _reformat_metrics(metric_dict)`) — confirms the reviewer's claim of upstream usage.

3. v2/figpack_curation.py is indeed a 3-line stub:
```
"""Interactive web-based curation views for sorted units."""
# Runtime implementation added by the matching spike-sorting v2 change.
```
Confirms the known-gap status.

4. grep across v2 source and tests/spikesorting/v2/ finds zero references to `_reformat_metrics` or `reformat_metrics` — the helper is truly absent in v2 and untested there.

Classification `missing_in_v2` with severity `low` is appropriate: the helper is absent because the FigURLCuration/figpack feature is deferred (documented as a known stub). It is not a regression in shipped functionality; it just needs to be ported when the feature is implemented. Severity low matches because the absent helper cannot cause silent miscomputation — calling figpack_curation would simply fail to do anything.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 CurationLabel enum vs v1 valid_labels list -- enforcement scope differs
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 declares `valid_labels = ['reject', 'noise', 'artifact', 'mua', 'accept']` as a module-level list at v1/curation.py:26. No automated enforcement at insert time; the list is documented in the `insert_curation` docstring.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 promotes the labels to a `CurationLabel(str, Enum)` enum (utils.py:50-66) with values `accept, mua, noise, artifact, reject`. Enforced at insert time inside `CurationV2.insert_curation` (curation.py:507-524). Docstring (utils.py:55-60) explicitly mentions free-form `dj.Manual.insert1` bypassing the helper remains permitted (DataJoint cannot enforce enums on varchar columns), and downstream filters fall back to the v1 list for unrecognized labels.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Labels match exactly (same five strings). Enforcement is a strict superset of v1; documented in the enum docstring. Test coverage exists: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:1876 and :3202.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Explicitly justified in utils.py:50-60 docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all reviewer claims at the cited lines.

v1 (v1/curation.py:26): `valid_labels = ["reject", "noise", "artifact", "mua", "accept"]` — module-level list, no automated enforcement, only documented in docstrings. Confirmed by direct read.

v2 (v2/utils.py:50-66): `class CurationLabel(str, Enum)` with members `accept, mua, noise, artifact, reject`. Same five strings as v1. Docstring at lines 51-60 explicitly states: "Members match the v1 convention list... v2 promotes the list from a docstring to an enforced enum so a typo raises at insert time. Free-form ``dj.Manual.insert1`` calls bypassing the helper remain permitted (DataJoint cannot enforce enums on varchar columns), and downstream filters fall back to the v1 list for any unrecognized label that slipped in." This matches the reviewer's documented rationale verbatim.

v2 enforcement (v2/curation.py:499-524): `_validate_labels` is a staticmethod that builds `valid = {member.value for member in CurationLabel}` and raises `ValueError` with message "label {lbl!r} for unit_id={unit_id} is not in CurationLabel. Valid labels: {sorted(valid)}." Reviewer cited lines 507-524; actual implementation spans 499-524 (close enough; cited range covers the validation core).

Test coverage confirmed:
- test_single_session_pipeline.py:1876 uses `pytest.raises(ValueError, match="not in CurationLabel")` with v1-era label `"good"` to verify rejection.
- test_single_session_pipeline.py:3202 imports `CurationLabel` and uses `CurationLabel.noise` for happy-path label insertion.

Classification: intentional_justified is correct — the divergence is a strict superset (same labels, added enforcement), explicitly documented in the enum docstring, and test-covered for both happy and failure paths. Severity low is appropriate: no behavioral regression for valid inputs; only stricter rejection of typos, which is the stated goal.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 introduces MetricsSource enum with no v1 analog; figpack member unused (no figpack code yet)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no MetricsSource concept. The provenance of curation metrics is implicit (CurationV1 stores a single metrics blob; downstream MetricCuration / FigURLCuration are separate tables with no provenance enum).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 introduces `MetricsSource(str, Enum)` (utils.py:36-47) with members `manual, analyzer_curation, figpack`. Used in CurationV2 (curation.py:141, 307-312) to validate the `metrics_source` column. `figpack` member is declared but v2/figpack_curation.py is a 3-line stub -- no code produces figpack-sourced metrics yet.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 SourceResolution dataclass is new, gating dispatch in Sorting.make / ArtifactDetection.make
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no source-part pattern; SpikeSortingSelection and ArtifactDetectionSelection FK upstream tables directly via plain columns, no dispatch.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 introduces a frozen dataclass `SourceResolution(kind, key)` (utils.py:127-144) with `kind` as a Literal of three source-part kinds (`recording`, `concatenated_recording`, `shared_artifact_group`). Used by `<Master>.resolve_source(key)` to dispatch which source-part backs a master.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Forward-declared support for ConcatenatedRecordingSource and SharedArtifactGroupSource. ConcatenatedRecording is itself a NotImplementedError-stubbed feature per the v2 known-gaps list, so the `concatenated_recording` kind is forward-declared. Has test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:667-677.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Docstring at utils.py:128-137 explicitly justifies the dataclass.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims:

1. **SourceResolution dataclass exists at v2/utils.py:127-144** as a frozen dataclass with the documented docstring (lines 129-137) explicitly stating its purpose: "Used at the top of Sorting.make() and ArtifactDetection.make() to dispatch on which source-part row backs the master." The `kind` field is exactly `Literal["recording", "concatenated_recording", "shared_artifact_group"]` and `key` is `dict`.

2. **v1 has no analog**: `grep -rn "SourceResolution\|resolve_source" v1/` returns no matches. v1's `SpikeSortingSelection` (v1/sorting.py:198-206) FKs `SpikeSortingRecording` directly via a plain column with no dispatch:
```
sorting_id: uuid
---
-> SpikeSortingRecording
-> SpikeSorterParameters
-> IntervalList
```

3. **v2 uses the pattern broadly**: 16+ call sites across `v2/sorting.py`, `v2/artifact.py`, and `v2/curation.py` use `resolve_source` / `SourceResolution`. The factory methods at `v2/sorting.py:369-403` and `v2/artifact.py:538-571` return `SourceResolution` instances. Master `.make()` and `.delete()` methods dispatch on the returned `kind`.

4. **Test coverage exists**: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:667-681 directly imports `SourceResolution` and asserts `isinstance(resolution, SourceResolution)`, `resolution.kind == "recording"`, and the key shape. Additional coverage at lines 2378-2402 (bypass detection), 5409-5450 (cleanup error path), and 8301-8326 (delete error path).

5. **Forward-declared kinds**: `concatenated_recording` and `shared_artifact_group` are part of the Literal type but `ConcatenatedRecording` is a v2 known-gap stub, so those branches are not yet exercised end-to-end — but the dataclass itself is fully tested for the `recording` case.

Classification: new_in_v2 is correct — this is a genuinely new v2 abstraction with no v1 equivalent, documented rationale, and test coverage. Severity low is appropriate: it's a structural addition supporting the schema-first source-part design, not a behavioral regression.

confirmed_findings.title: v1's get_spiking_sorting_v1_merge_ids has duplicated/dead branch; v2's get_spiking_sorting_v2_merge_ids delegates to merge table
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 helper (utils.py:37-109) has obviously copy-pasted dead code: lines 79-91 contain `if ... else ...` where BOTH branches are identical (`SpikeSortingSelection() & sorting_restriction & rec_dict`). The 'sorted without artifact detection' branch is unreachable because both queries are textually the same. When curation_id is unspecified, returns latest via `np.max(...)` per sorting_id.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 helper (utils.py:536-568) is a thin wrapper over `SpikeSortingOutput()._get_restricted_merge_ids_v2(restriction, as_dict=as_dict)`. Adds an `as_dict` parameter (default False matching v1's plain-list return). v2's helper validates unknown keys (per docstring: 'Unknown keys raise ValueError').
confirmed_findings.evidence: v1 dead-branch is a real bug (the 'no artifact detection' fallback is unreachable). v2's delegation to `_get_restricted_merge_ids_v2` is cleaner and validated. v2 helper has parity test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:297-300. The `as_dict` addition is explicitly explained in the docstring as an enhancement that preserves v1 default behavior.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented in v2/utils.py:541-562 docstring.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly in source.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/utils.py:78-91):
```
        # if sorted with artifact detection
        if SpikeSortingSelection() & sorting_restriction & rec_dict:
            sorting_id_list.append(
                (
                    SpikeSortingSelection() & sorting_restriction & rec_dict
                ).fetch1("sorting_id")
            )
        # if sorted without artifact detection
        else:
            sorting_id_list.append(
                (
                    SpikeSortingSelection() & sorting_restriction & rec_dict
                ).fetch1("sorting_id")
            )
```
Both branches are textually identical. The "if sorted without artifact detection" branch is unreachable — a real (silent) dead-branch bug. Also confirmed v1 uses np.max(...) over curation_id at line 97-100 when curation_id is unspecified.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/utils.py:536-568): thin wrapper over `SpikeSortingOutput()._get_restricted_merge_ids_v2(restriction, as_dict=as_dict)`. Default `as_dict=False` preserves v1's plain-list return. Docstring at lines 539-562 explicitly references v1 location and explains the as_dict enhancement.

Test coverage: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:294-302 confirms signature parity (restriction + as_dict params present). Behavioral coverage of the underlying _get_restricted_merge_ids_v2 exists in tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2249-2282 (test_get_restricted_merge_ids_v2_resolves_through_chain) and tests/spikesorting/v2/test_legacy_runtime_boundary.py:212. The helper itself only has the signature-existence test — its delegation behavior is covered indirectly.

Classification intentional_justified is correct: v2 explicitly documents the change in docstring, the as_dict default preserves v1 behavior, and v2 fixes a v1 dead-branch bug by delegating to a validated merge-table method. Severity medium is appropriate — v1's dead branch silently does the right thing because both queries are identical, but the intended "no artifact detection" semantic was never actually implemented (latent correctness issue if a user ever needed that fallback).

confirmed_findings.title: v2 unit_brain_region_df returns empty df with full schema; v1 has no analog
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: (none -- no v1 analog at all; v1 attached brain regions inline)
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 `unit_brain_region_df(unit_relation, resolution)` (utils.py:94-124) joins Unit x Electrode x BrainRegion, returns DataFrame with columns `[unit_id, electrode_id, region_name, subregion_name, subsubregion_name, region_resolution]`. confirmed_findings.title: v2 _assert_v2_db_safe is import-time guard against non-test DB; v1 has no such guard
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no DB-host safety check; v1 schemas register against whatever `dj.config['database.host']` points to.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 declares `_assert_v2_db_safe()` (utils.py:151-199) that hard-fails import unless `database.host in {localhost, 127.0.0.1, ::1}` or env var `SPYGLASS_SPIKESORTING_V2_ALLOW_NONLOCAL_DB=1` is set. Called at module-import time from every v2 schema module (recording.py:40, sorting.py, artifact.py:55, curation.py:35, session_group.py:32).
confirmed_findings.evidence: Safety guard for in-development v2. The override env name and behavior is documented (utils.py:177-182). No test directly exercises the guard (grep in tests returns zero hits for `_assert_v2_db_safe`), but the env var SPYGLASS_SPIKESORTING_V2_ALLOW_NONLOCAL_DB itself is not referenced in tests either -- the guard's negative path (refuse-non-local + raise) is untested.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Explicitly justified in utils.py:156-182 docstring as a temporary safety net for active v2 development.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims by direct read.

(1) v2 utils.py:151-199 declares `_SAFE_DB_HOSTS = frozenset({"localhost", "127.0.0.1", "::1"})`, `_OVERRIDE_ENV = "SPYGLASS_SPIKESORTING_V2_ALLOW_NONLOCAL_DB"`, and `_assert_v2_db_safe()` which returns early if the env var is "1" or if `dj.config['database.host']` is in `_SAFE_DB_HOSTS`, else raises `RuntimeError`. Code matches the reviewer's summary exactly.

(2) Import-time calls verified at the cited locations:
- recording.py:40 _assert_v2_db_safe()
- sorting.py:71 _assert_v2_db_safe() (reviewer cited "sorting.py" without a line; confirmed present)
- artifact.py:55 _assert_v2_db_safe()
- curation.py:35 _assert_v2_db_safe()
- session_group.py:32 _assert_v2_db_safe()

(3) `grep -rn "_SAFE_DB_HOSTS\|database.host" src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/*.py` returns zero hits — v1 has no analogous guard. Claim of no v1 analog holds.

(4) Rationale is explicit in utils.py:156-182 docstring: "While the v2 pipeline is under active development we hard-fail any attempt to declare or write to v2 schemas on a database other than the isolated test docker container." The override env var and its risks are documented in the Override section.

(5) `grep -rn "_assert_v2_db_safe\|SPYGLASS_SPIKESORTING_V2_ALLOW_NONLOCAL_DB" tests/` returns zero hits. The guard's negative-path (non-local host → raise) and override path are untested.

Classification new_in_v2 is correct: v2 added a safety guard with no v1 analog, intentionally and with documented rationale. Severity medium is reasonable — the guard protects against destructive writes to a production DB during active development, but the refuse-and-raise path itself is untested, so a regression in the host comparison or env-var handling could silently fail open without detection in CI.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 introduces _resolved_job_kwargs priority-merge with three sources; v1 just took raw job_kwargs
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 took `job_kwargs` dict directly from caller and passed straight into ChunkRecordingExecutor / SI compute (no SI-global merge, no dj.config merge).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 `_resolved_job_kwargs(*row_job_kwargs)` (utils.py:297-322) merges in increasing precedence: SI global defaults -> `dj.config['custom']['spikesorting_v2_job_kwargs']` -> per-row blobs in order given. Empty/None entries skipped.
confirmed_findings.v1_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py (passes job_kwargs directly to run_sorter / ChunkRecordingExecutor)
confirmed_findings.evidence: Well-tested: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_scaffold.py:69-93 exercises precedence rules including None/{} handling and multi-row override merge.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented at utils.py:298-303.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all four claims against actual code:

1. v2 code at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/utils.py:297-322 matches the claim exactly:
```
def _resolved_job_kwargs(*row_job_kwargs: dict | None) -> dict:
    ...
    merged = dict(si.get_global_job_kwargs())
    custom = dj.config.get("custom", {}) or {}
    merged.update(custom.get("spikesorting_v2_job_kwargs", {}) or {})
    for override in row_job_kwargs:
        if override:
            merged.update(override)
    return merged
```
Precedence: SI-global -> dj.config custom -> per-row blobs (in order). Empty/None entries skipped (`if override:` guard).

2. Rationale documented in docstring at utils.py:298-303: "Sources are merged in increasing precedence order: the SpikeInterface global defaults, then ``dj.config['custom']['spikesorting_v2_job_kwargs']``, then each per-row blob in the order given."

3. v1 behavior confirmed: v1/sorting.py uses no job_kwargs at all (run_sorter at lines 446, 452 passes sorter_params directly). v1/artifact.py:218 receives `**job_kwargs` and passes them through unchanged to ChunkRecordingExecutor at line 304: `**job_kwargs`. No SI-global merge, no dj.config merge in v1.

4. Test coverage verified at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_scaffold.py:69-93 — `test_resolved_job_kwargs_merge` exercises: SI-global passthrough (lines 82-84), dj.config override of SI global (line 79), per-row blob overrides dj.config (line 87), later per-row arg wins over earlier (line 89), None skipped (line 91), empty dict skipped (line 93).

Classification new_in_v2 is correct — this is a new utility with no v1 equivalent. Severity low is appropriate: additive feature, documented rationale, well-tested, per-row blob remains highest precedence so v1 caller semantics aren't silently overridden by global config.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 transaction_or_noop wraps source-part inserts but v1 had no equivalent; new contract assumed by callers
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 had no atomicity helper for master+part inserts; individual `insert_selection` methods relied on DataJoint's default per-call autocommit.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 `transaction_or_noop` (utils.py:19-33) wraps master + part inserts in one transaction unless already in one. CurationV2.insert_curation has an enforced contract: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:785-881 statically (AST) verifies `insert_curation` uses `transaction_or_noop` and forbids certain inserts outside the block.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Well-justified (utils.py:21-28 docstring explains nested-transaction issue) and well-tested via AST-walk test at test_v1_parity.py:836-881.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented in utils.py:21-28.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all three pillars of the claim by reading the actual code:

1) v2 helper exists as described. `transaction_or_noop` is defined at /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/utils.py:19-33 with the documented behavior:
```
if connection.in_transaction:
    yield
else:
    with connection.transaction:
        yield
```
The docstring at utils.py:21-28 explicitly explains the nested-transaction motivation: "source-part inserts and curation inserts both want to wrap their master + part rows in one transaction; but the same helpers may be called from inside an existing populate cascade where DataJoint refuses nested transactions."

2) v1 has NO equivalent. `grep -rn "transaction\|in_transaction\|contextmanager" src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/` returns zero hits. v1's `CurationV1.insert_curation` (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:44-128) uses a bare `cls.insert1(key, skip_duplicates=True)` at line 126 with no transaction wrapping. NWB writes and master inserts happen serially relying on DataJoint's per-call autocommit.

3) v2 adoption is broad. The helper is used at 10+ call sites: curation.py:459, artifact.py:358/496/824, recording.py:398/556/1075, sorting.py:343/667 — covering all master+source-part insert paths and the manual curation insert.

4) The AST-walk test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:780-881 statically verifies that CurationV2.insert_curation uses `transaction_or_noop` and that no NWB-write call (`_stage_curated_units_nwb`, `AnalysisNwbfile().create`, `pynwb.NWBHDF5IO`) appears INSIDE the transaction block. Lines 867-871 assert at least one `transaction_or_noop` with-block must exist; lines 874-884 forbid NWB writes inside it.

Classification `new_in_v2` is correct: this is a brand-new helper with no v1 analog. Severity `medium` is appropriate — it is a correctness-positive addition (atomic master+part inserts; structural-test-enforced contract) rather than a regression, with clear documented justification in the docstring.

confirmed_findings.title: SessionGroup.create_group is unimplemented; documented contract for multi-day + motion-correction enforcement is unenforced
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: No v1 equivalent — there is no cross-session grouping table in v1.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: SessionGroup.create_group raises NotImplementedError unconditionally. The class docstring at v2/session_group.py:46-49 explicitly promises 'multi-day requires allow_multi_day=True AND forces an explicit MotionCorrectionParameters row (see create_group)', but the helper that would enforce this is just a stub.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/session_group.py:84-86 raises 'SessionGroup.create_group is not yet implemented'. The Manual master can still be insert1'd directly by a user, bypassing every invariant promised by the docstring (same-day default, multi-day gating, mandatory motion params, member-date derivation). No test exercises this path: grep across tests/spikesorting/v2/ returns zero hits for 'SessionGroup.create_group' or 'is_multi_day'.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Module docstring v2/session_group.py:1-7 cites a zero-migration policy: schema is final-shape, populate/insert bodies are gated until materializer lands. This is justified for downstream FK stability but leaves the user-facing invariant unenforced.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims against the actual code.

1. v2/session_group.py:69-86 contains `create_group` which raises `NotImplementedError("SessionGroup.create_group is not yet implemented")` unconditionally — matches reviewer claim exactly.

2. The class docstring at lines 46-49 reads: "Same-day groups are the default; multi-day requires ``allow_multi_day=True`` AND forces an explicit ``MotionCorrectionParameters`` row (see ``create_group``)." This contract is real and unenforced.

3. `is_multi_day` at lines 88-96 is also a stub raising NotImplementedError.

4. `SessionGroup` is declared as `dj.Manual` with no custom `insert1` override, so direct `SessionGroup.insert1(...)` and `SessionGroup.Member.insert1(...)` will succeed and bypass every invariant the docstring promises (same-day default, multi-day gating, mandatory motion params, member-date derivation).

5. No v1 equivalent: grep for "SessionGroup\|create_group" in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/ returned zero hits. Correctly classified as new_in_v2.

6. No tests exercise this path: grep for "create_group" in tests/spikesorting/v2/ matched only `SortedSpikesGroup().create_group` calls (in test_downstream_consumers.py and test_single_session_pipeline.py), which is a different class. No matches for `SessionGroup.create_group` or `is_multi_day`.

7. Documented rationale is real: module docstring v2/session_group.py:1-7 cites zero-migration policy ("schema is frozen; the populate body of ``ConcatenatedRecording`` is gated behind ``NotImplementedError`` until the concat materializer lands"). A dedicated `SessionGroupDateError` exception class at v2/exceptions.py:39 is even reserved for `create_group()`.

Classification new_in_v2 is correct. Severity: I downgraded from high to medium. Justifications for medium rather than high: (a) the downstream consumer `ConcatenatedRecording.make()` and `ConcatenatedRecordingSelection.insert_selection` are also gated with NotImplementedError, so direct inserts cannot propagate into computed results today; (b) the zero-migration rationale is explicitly documented in the module docstring; (c) `MotionCorrectionParameters.insert1` does have real Pydantic validation already wired. The gap is real (users CAN insert bypassing the contract), but the blast radius is contained because the consumer chain is gated.

confirmed_findings.title: ConcatenatedRecordingSelection.insert_selection is a stub — Manual table is writable but no validation gate exists
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: No v1 equivalent.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: insert_selection raises NotImplementedError. The class docstring at v2/session_group.py:158-160 promises 'enforces that every member has a populated Recording row matching the requested preprocessing parameters', but only via the unimplemented helper. Because the table is dj.Manual a user can still call insert1() and create a ConcatenatedRecordingSelection row without any Recording-coverage check.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Body is just 'raise NotImplementedError(...)'. SortingSelection.insert_selection at v2/sorting.py:271-276 rejects concat_recording_id with NotImplementedError, but that only blocks the Sorting fork; ConcatenatedRecordingSelection itself can still be populated by a direct insert1 bypassing MissingRecordingForConcatError (defined at v2/exceptions.py:58-63 but never raised).
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Module docstring v2/session_group.py:1-7 — zero-migration policy. The schema is intentionally final-shape; consumer logic comes later.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the cited code directly:

1. `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/session_group.py:153` declares `class ConcatenatedRecordingSelection(SpyglassMixin, dj.Manual)` — confirmed it is a `dj.Manual`, so `insert1()` is writable by default DataJoint semantics.

2. Lines 171-181: `insert_selection` body is exactly `raise NotImplementedError("ConcatenatedRecordingSelection.insert_selection is not yet implemented")`. The docstring at lines 173-177 says "Forward-declared; the schema is in place so single-session tables can FK ``concat_recording_id`` from day one."

3. The class docstring at lines 154-161 does state the helper "when implemented, enforces that every member has a populated ``Recording`` row matching the requested preprocessing parameters."

4. `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/exceptions.py:58-63` defines `MissingRecordingForConcatError` with docstring referencing `ConcatenatedRecordingSelection.insert_selection()`. A grep of `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/` shows it is NEVER raised — only declared.

5. `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:271-276` confirms `SortingSelection.insert_selection` rejects `concat_recording_id` with `NotImplementedError("...concatenated recording sorting is not implemented yet...")`.

6. Tests: only `test_sorting_selection_rejects_concat_source` at `tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:5082` tests the gate on `SortingSelection`. No test exercises `ConcatenatedRecordingSelection.insert_selection` or attempts a direct `insert1()` to confirm rejection. The direct-insert bypass branch is untested.

7. No v1 equivalent for concatenated recordings — this is genuinely new_in_v2.

Module docstring (lines 1-7) documents the rationale: zero-migration policy means the schema ships in final shape while consumer logic comes later — both `make()` and the FK-using `SortingSelection` paths are gated.

Classification holds: `new_in_v2`. On severity, I would soften from "high" to "medium": although the gate on the helper is real, ALL downstream consumers (`SortingSelection` rejects concat at sorting.py:271-276, `ConcatenatedRecording.make()` raises NotImplementedError at session_group.py:223-225) also raise NotImplementedError. So a user who calls `insert1()` directly creates an orphan row but cannot cascade it into any computed table or scientific output. The risk is documentation/promise drift and future-violation surface area, not present scientific incorrectness. Hence medium, not high.

confirmed_findings.title: ConcatenatedRecording.make body unimplemented — downstream FK target is permanently empty
