The v2 shipping default mirrors that choice." `schema_version=3` is explicit: "3 made noise_levels optional."

v2 shipped row (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:166-176): Explicitly passes `{"noise_levels": [1.0]}` with a 10-line comment justifying that this mirrors v1's microvolt interpretation, while smoke/synthetic rows OMIT it so SI computes per-channel MAD.

v2 runtime (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1146-1176): Branches on `nl_in is None`. None path pops the key (SI MAD fallback) AND pins `random_slices_kwargs={"seed": 0}` to compensate for SI PR #3359 making per-channel MAD non-deterministic (lines 1151-1167) — a non-obvious correctness fix. Else path broadcasts scalar noise level across channels (lines 1168-1176).

The finding's classification (intentional_justified) is fully supported by extensive in-code documentation at three sites (schema docstring, shipped-row comment, runtime comment). The semantic shift the finding flags — custom rows OMITTING noise_levels now get SI MAD whereas in v1 omission was impossible — is real and is explicitly the documented intent (lines 137-145). Severity 'high' is justified given the prior 1400× clusterless divergence from a Pydantic mutable-default bug; this change carefully avoids that class of bug (None default, not [1.0] default) while preserving end-user behavior on the shipped preset and enabling MAD-based detection for synthetic/low-amplitude fixtures.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 drops v1 preprocessing fields 'margin_ms' and 'seed' from the schema
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: SpikeSortingPreprocessingParameters 'default' row contains margin_ms=5 and seed=0 in its params blob. v1's filter_params accessor reads only frequency_min/frequency_max (recording.py:623-624), so margin_ms and seed are never actually consumed — they are documented but inert.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: PreprocessingParamsSchema has no margin_ms field and no seed field; the seed for the externalized whitening is hard-coded to 0 (overrideable via job_kwargs.random_seed) at v2/sorting.py:1281.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v1's margin_ms and seed were dead fields — confirmed by grep on v1/recording.py: only filter_params['frequency_min'] and filter_params['frequency_max'] are read. v2 removes them so the schema does not document fields the runtime ignores. Seed has been migrated to the per-row job_kwargs blob.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Schema-first design rationale: 'Removed CommonReferenceParams.reference -- dead field' documented in PreprocessingParamsSchema docstring (_params/preprocessing.py:88-93). Same principle applies to margin_ms/seed though it is not called out as explicitly.

verdict.reasoning:
All claims in the finding hold up under direct code inspection.

(1) v1 default row at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:127-138 contains `"margin_ms": 5` and `"seed": 0` in the params blob.

(2) v1's filter_params accessor at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:621-626 only consumes `filter_params["frequency_min"]` and `filter_params["frequency_max"]`:
    recording = si.preprocessing.bandpass_filter(
        recording,
        freq_min=filter_params["frequency_min"],
        freq_max=filter_params["frequency_max"],
        dtype=np.float64,
    )
Grep across v1/recording.py confirms `margin_ms` appears only in the definition (line 112) and default contents (line 133); the `"seed"` string only appears in line 134. Neither is read anywhere in v1 preprocessing — they are inert in v1. (The other `seed` hits returned by grep are in v1/metric_curation.py, which belongs to the MetricParameters table, not preprocessing.)

(3) v2's PreprocessingParamsSchema at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/preprocessing.py:74-122 has fields {schema_version, bandpass_filter, common_reference, whiten, min_segment_length}. No `margin_ms`, no `seed`. Grep confirms `margin_ms` is entirely absent from src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/.

(4) v2 whitening seed at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1281 is pinned to 0, overrideable via job_kwargs.random_seed:
    _random_seed = (job_kwargs or {}).get("random_seed", 0)
    recording = sip.whiten(recording, dtype=_np.float64, seed=_random_seed)
The surrounding comment block (lines 1263-1280) explicitly documents the seed migration and the reason (SI PR #3359 changed default seed from 0 to None).

(5) Classification of intentional_justified is correct: v2 removes schema fields that v1 documented but never consumed (schema-first design principle), and migrates seed to a per-row job_kwargs knob with documented rationale. Severity low is appropriate — no behavioral change since the fields were dead in v1.

One minor caveat (does not refute the finding): the PreprocessingParamsSchema docstring at lines 88-93 only enumerates the schema_version=2 changes "added min_segment_length" and "removed CommonReferenceParams.reference". It does not explicitly call out the removal of `margin_ms` and `seed` from the v1 blob, which is a documentation gap rather than a correctness issue. The finding itself acknowledges this ("though it is not called out as explicitly").

confirmed_findings.title: v2 renames v1 keys frequency_min/frequency_max → freq_min/freq_max
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 default preprocessing row uses keys 'frequency_min' and 'frequency_max' (v1/recording.py:131-132) and reads them via filter_params['frequency_min'] (v1/recording.py:623).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 PreprocessingParamsSchema.BandpassFilterParams uses keys 'freq_min' and 'freq_max' (matching SI's sip.bandpass_filter signature). Custom v1 rows would fail Pydantic validation on insert into v2.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 schema has extra='forbid' (BandpassFilterParams ConfigDict), so a v1-style blob with 'frequency_min' would raise at insert. v2 also names the fields to match SpikeInterface's sip.bandpass_filter signature.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: BandpassFilterParams docstring at _params/preprocessing.py:9-19 documents that v1 always REQUIRED the keys and v2 promotes them to schema-level defaults; the rename to match SI is implicit but consistent with extra='forbid' policy.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all cited lines directly.

v1/recording.py:131-132 confirms the default row uses 'frequency_min'/'frequency_max':
    "frequency_min": 300,  # high pass filter value
    "frequency_max": 6000,  # low pass filter value

v1/recording.py:623-624 confirms v1 reads these keys from the blob:
    freq_min=filter_params["frequency_min"],
    freq_max=filter_params["frequency_max"],
(v1 itself translates to SI's freq_min/freq_max API at the call site.)

v2/_params/preprocessing.py:21-23 confirms the rename plus extra='forbid':
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")
    freq_min: float = Field(default=300.0, ge=0.0, le=15000.0)
    freq_max: float = Field(default=6000.0, ge=0.0, le=15000.0)

The docstring at lines 9-19 explicitly documents the rationale: 'v1 had no standalone schema defaults (frequency_min / frequency_max were always required keys on the params blob); v2 promotes them to schema-level defaults...' The new names match SpikeInterface's sip.bandpass_filter signature, which v1 was already adapting to at runtime.

Behavioral consequence: a v1-style blob inserted into v2 raises pydantic ValidationError (extra fields forbidden + missing required-by-name freq_min/freq_max if user passes the v1 names). This is a fail-loud break, not silent divergence. Defaults preserve v1's production values (300/6000), so the default workflow is unaffected. Classification intentional_justified and severity medium both hold.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 CommonReferenceParams drops v1's 'reference' field and exposes 'operator' as a user knob
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's preprocessing default row has no 'reference' field but the runtime dispatches based on sort_reference_electrode_id only (recording.py:597-619): -1 = no reference, -2 = global median, positive = single-channel. operator='median' is hardcoded at recording.py:611.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2 drops the inert 'reference' field entirely (extra='forbid' rejects it). 'operator' is exposed as a Literal['median', 'average'] with default 'median' — passing 'average' is a v2-only capability.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v1's runtime never read a 'reference' field from filter_params (grep confirms no 'reference' read in v1/recording.py); v2 documents this as 'Promoted from silent runtime override to field removed so the schema does not lie about what the runtime honors' (_params/preprocessing.py:46-47). v2 operator default 'median' matches v1's hardcoded behavior.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented at _params/preprocessing.py:38-57.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by direct code reading.

v1 default preproc_params row (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:127-138) contains only frequency_min/frequency_max/margin_ms/seed/min_segment_length — NO 'reference' field. The default row never carried it.

v1 runtime dispatch (recording.py:597-619) branches purely on ref_channel_id (the sort_reference_electrode_id column, not filter_params):
- `if ref_channel_id >= 0`: `reference="single"`, ref_channel_ids=ref_channel_id
- `elif ref_channel_id == -2`: `reference="global"`, `operator="median"` HARDCODED at line 611
- `elif ref_channel_id != -1`: raises ValueError

Grep of "reference" in v1/recording.py confirms no read of a 'reference' key from filter_params anywhere — every occurrence is either (a) string-literal kwargs to si.preprocessing.common_reference, (b) the sort_reference_electrode_id column comment at line 40-41, or (c) the unrelated `references` dict for sort-group config (line 54-77, electrode_group -> reference_electrode mapping).

v2 schema (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/preprocessing.py:35-56):
- Line 55: `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")` — passing 'reference' raises ValidationError.
- Line 56: `operator: Literal["median", "average"] = "median"` — operator is now a user knob, default matches v1's hardcoded 'median'.
- Docstring lines 38-52 explicitly document the decision: "The `reference` field of v1's preprocessing params is intentionally not exposed in v2 because no production v1 workflow used it -- v1 hardcoded the same dispatch... Promoted from 'silent runtime override' to 'field removed' so the schema does not lie about what the runtime honors."
- Schema_version bumped to 2 at line 96 with rationale at lines 88-93 listing "removed CommonReferenceParams.reference (dead field)" as one of the schema-incompatible edits.

Classification holds: intentional_justified. Default operator='median' preserves v1 behavior; 'average' is opt-in and only takes effect on the global-median branch (ref_channel_id == -2), which is itself a user-configured choice. No regression risk for v1-equivalent configs. Severity low is appropriate.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema rejects detect=True with both thresholds None; v1 silently skips
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _get_artifact_times (v1/artifact.py:256-264) checks `if amplitude_thresh_uV is zscore_thresh is None` and silently SKIPS detection at runtime, returning the unmasked recording. v1's 'none' preset (v1/artifact.py:78-87) relies on this fall-through.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema._check_thresholds (v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:73-80) raises ValueError at insert time if detect=True and both thresholds are None. The 'none' preset works via an explicit detect=False field (v2/artifact.py:118-120).
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 promotes a runtime fall-through into an explicit typed field — schema-first gating, consistent with the noise_levels=[1.0] lesson. v2 'none' preset functionally matches v1 'none' (no scanning, full session is valid time).
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Implicit from the 'detect=False' design + extra='forbid' on the schema; no inline rationale comment beyond field naming.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims at the cited lines:

**v1 silent-skip behavior confirmed** at `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/artifact.py:255-264`:
```python
# if both thresholds are None, we skip artifract detection
if amplitude_thresh_uV is zscore_thresh is None:
    if verbose:
        logger.info(
            "Amplitude and zscore thresholds are both None, "
            + "skipping artifact detection"
        )
    return np.asarray(
        [valid_timestamps[0], valid_timestamps[-1]]
    ), np.asarray([])
```
This is a runtime fall-through: returns the full session as one valid interval, no detection.

**v1 'none' preset confirmed** at `v1/artifact.py:78-87` — both `zscore_thresh` and `amplitude_thresh_uV` are None, with no `detect` flag, relying on the runtime fall-through above.

**v2 schema-level rejection confirmed** at `src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:73-80`:
```python
@model_validator(mode="after")
def _check_thresholds(self) -> "ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema":
    if self.detect and self.amplitude_thresh_uV is None and self.zscore_thresh is None:
        raise ValueError(
            "ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema requires at least one of "
            "amplitude_thresh_uV or zscore_thresh when detect=True"
        )
    return self
```
The validator only fires when `detect=True`; with `detect=False` it returns cleanly.

**v2 'detect' field confirmed** at `v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:53` as `detect: bool = True`, and the schema has `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")` at line 36 — so the "none" preset MUST use an explicit typed field rather than absent thresholds.

**v2 'none' preset confirmed** at `v2/artifact.py:117-120`:
```python
"none",
ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema(
    detect=False, amplitude_thresh_uV=None
).model_dump(),
```
Constructs the schema with `detect=False` explicitly, bypassing the validator.

**Functional equivalence**: v1 "none" returns `[t_start, t_end]` as one valid interval and `[]` artifacts. v2 "none" with `detect=False` should produce the same effect (no scanning, whole session valid) — though I did not trace the v2 detect=False codepath in `artifact.py` end-to-end to verify the returned valid times match exactly. The finding's claim of functional equivalence is plausible but not exhaustively verified at the make()-level here.

**Classification**: intentional_justified is correct. The schema docstring at v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:18-21 explicitly calls out the design choice: "detect (so the "none" preset is a typed detect=False rather than an absent-fields blob)". This is documented rationale at the schema level — promoting an implicit runtime fall-through into an explicit typed gate. Aligns with the noise_levels=[1.0] lesson (schema-first defaults prevent silent divergence).

**Severity**: low is appropriate. The behavior change only affects users who construct ad-hoc params with `detect=True, amplitude_thresh_uV=None, zscore_thresh=None` — v1 would silently skip detection (likely unintended by the user); v2 raises with a clear message. This is a strict improvement at the contract level, not a regression. The "none" preset itself works identically from the caller's perspective.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 adds min_length_s field to artifact schema (was hardcoded in v1)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's _get_artifact_times calls sort_interval_valid_times.subtract(artifact_intervals_s, min_length=1) (v1/artifact.py:327-328) — the 1-second minimum length is hardcoded with no user override.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema.min_length_s: float = Field(default=1.0, gt=0.0). Default 1.0 matches v1's hardcoded value; users can override per-row.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Default value matches v1 hardcoded behavior; field promotion from constant to schema-tunable knob. Documented at _params/artifact_detection.py:37-43 (also the explicit reason for schema_version bump to 2).
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented at _params/artifact_detection.py:37-43.

verdict.reasoning:
All four cited claims verified directly in source.

1. v1/artifact.py:327-328 confirmed: `sort_interval_valid_times.subtract(artifact_intervals_s, min_length=1).union_consolidate()` — hardcoded `min_length=1`, no user override.

2. v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:71 confirmed: `min_length_s: float = Field(default=1.0, gt=0.0)` — schema-tunable with default matching v1's hardcoded value.

3. v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:37-43 confirmed: `schema_version: int = 2` with explicit comment "Bumped to 2 by adding ``min_length_s``: the artifact-removed valid_times are filtered to drop slivers shorter than this many seconds before the sorter sees them. Default ``1.0`` matches v1's hardcoded value at ``src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/artifact.py:327-328``."

4. v2/artifact.py:998-1003 confirmed the field is actually consumed: `kept = [[start, end] for start, end in kept if (end - start) >= validated.min_length_s]` with comment again explicitly cross-referencing v1's hardcoded `min_length=1`.

The schema_version bump is explicitly justified by this single addition; documentation is precise (cites v1 line numbers); default preserves v1 behavior. Minor implementation detail: v1 delegates to `Interval.subtract(min_length=...)` while v2 implements an inline filter with `>=`, but both have the same semantics at the default value. This is a textbook intentional, documented, low-risk field promotion from hardcoded constant to user-tunable knob.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 adds join_window_ms field with no v1 equivalent
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 has no concept of joining nearby artifact intervals — removal_window_ms is the only window-shaping knob (v1/artifact.py:324 via add_removal_window).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema.join_window_ms: float = Field(default=1.0, ge=0.0) — described as 'artifact intervals closer than this are merged into one'. This is a NEW field with no v1 default.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Field is new in v2; no v1 equivalent in default rows or in _get_artifact_times. The default 1.0 ms is a new behavior, not a port. Could change artifact masks (merging two close artifacts into one and thus changing the boundary of the masked-out interval) relative to v1 even on identical inputs.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: No inline rationale comment in the schema for the choice of 1.0 ms specifically. Field is mentioned in the schema docstring (_params/artifact_detection.py:21) as a v2 addition but no quantitative justification.

verdict.reasoning:
Finding holds up under direct code inspection.

(1) v2 schema field at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:70 reads `join_window_ms: float = Field(default=1.0, ge=0.0)`. Schema docstring at lines 18-21 calls it "artifact intervals closer than this are merged into one" and explicitly labels it a v2 addition.

(2) v1 has NO equivalent. Both v1 presets at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/artifact.py:65-87 (`default` and `none`) contain only `zscore_thresh`, `amplitude_thresh_uV`, `proportion_above_thresh`, `removal_window_ms`, `chunk_duration`, `n_jobs`, `progress_bar`. The `_get_artifact_times` signature at lines 210-218 has no join_window parameter. v1's only "merging" is implicit, via `Interval(artifact_frames, from_inds=True).add_removal_window(removal_window_ms, valid_timestamps)` at line 322-324: `from_inds` (common_interval.py:508-526) groups only CONSECUTIVE frames; `add_removal_window` (common_interval.py:918-937) then pads each interval by `removal_window_ms/2` per side and calls `_union_consolidate`, which merges only when the padded windows actually overlap.

(3) v2 implements an EXPLICIT join step BEFORE padding at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/artifact.py:944-960: it computes `join_window_frames = ceil(join_window_ms * 1e-3 * fs)` and merges any two threshold-crossing frames with `f - cur_end <= join_window_frames`, then applies `half_window_frames` padding around the joined spans.

(4) Behavioral divergence is real but smaller than the finding implies when defaults are aligned. With both `join_window_ms = removal_window_ms = 1.0`, v1's implicit merge condition (gap <= removal_window_ms in frames so padded intervals overlap) and v2's explicit join condition (gap <= join_window_frames) coincide, and the final padded span [N1 - half, N2 + half] is identical. The divergence becomes meaningful only when a user sets `join_window_ms != removal_window_ms`, or when v1's frame-overlap threshold uses a strict `<` and v2's join uses `<=` (or vice versa) at the boundary.

(5) Test coverage exists: tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:4260 (`test_detect_artifacts_join_window_merges_runs`) and tests/spikesorting/v2/test_params_validation.py:84 verify the field. The parity layer at tests/spikesorting/v2/_parity_canonical.py:309-343 EXPLICITLY documents the convention: "Filling v2 defaults on the v1 side is the 'v1's absent fields silently followed the v2 schema defaults' reading", canonicalizing absent v1 `join_window_ms` to 1.0. This is a deliberate equivalence fiction, not literal v1 behavior.

(6) Justification: schema acknowledges the addition but no quantitative rationale for `1.0 ms` is given inline. No CHANGELOG entry mentions join_window specifically.

Classification: new_in_v2 (clearly a new field with no v1 analog). Severity remains medium because (a) defaults are chosen so that the typical case still matches v1's implicit behavior, (b) test coverage exists, but (c) non-default configs change artifact-mask boundaries and the 1.0 ms default has no documented justification.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 promotes proportion_above_thresh constraints from runtime warnings to insert-time errors
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's _check_artifact_thresholds (utils.py:242-249) warns and silently clamps proportion_above_thresh to 0.01 if <0, and clamps to 1.0 if >1. Runtime fallback, no insert-time rejection.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: ArtifactDetectionParamsSchema.proportion_above_thresh: float = Field(default=1.0, gt=0.0, le=1.0) — rejects 0.0 and >1.0 at INSERT time (not runtime). Stricter contract.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Stricter constraint moved from runtime to schema. Defaults match (1.0 in both). The constraint gt=0.0 (vs v1's silent 0.01 floor) is a tighter contract — v2 would reject 0.0 exactly, v1 silently clamped. Schema-first design lesson from the noise_levels=[1.0] bug class.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Implicit: schema-first design philosophy. No inline rationale comment.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against both files.

v1 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/utils.py:242-256:
```
if proportion_above_thresh < 0:
    warnings.warn(... "Using proportion_above_thresh = 0.01 instead of ...")
    proportion_above_thresh = 0.01
elif proportion_above_thresh > 1:
    warnings.warn(... "Using proportion_above_thresh = 1 instead of ...")
    proportion_above_thresh = 1
```
This is a runtime warn-and-clamp, not an insert-time check.

v2 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_params/artifact_detection.py:68:
```
proportion_above_thresh: float = Field(default=1.0, gt=0.0, le=1.0)
```
Pydantic rejects values <= 0.0 or > 1.0 at model construction (insert-time), with default 1.0 matching v1's clamp ceiling.

The finding holds. One small refinement: v1 uses strict `< 0`, so v1 actually accepts `0.0` unclamped (degenerate but allowed); v2's `gt=0.0` rejects exact `0.0`. This makes v2 marginally stricter than the finding's summary, but does not change the classification.

Rationale for intentional_justified: the surrounding class docstring (lines 8-34) and the inline default comment at lines 46-48 explicitly justify the 1.0 default as v1's "principled all-channels-must-exceed" value and frame the schema as the validated parameter blob. No inline rationale on the specific gt=0.0/le=1.0 bounds, but the schema-first philosophy is documented at the class level, consistent with v2's stated correctness-first port goal and the noise_levels=[1.0] bug-class lesson.

Severity low is correct: any well-formed v1 call with proportion in (0, 1] behaves identically in v2. Only previously-tolerated degenerate values (0.0, negatives, > 1) now hard-fail at insert instead of being silently clamped with a warning. No silent numerical divergence — bad params fail loudly.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 Kilosort4Schema curates a fixed default set; v1 dynamically uses sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1's SpikeSorterParameters.contents.extend uses `sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)` for every installed SI sorter including KS4 (v1/sorting.py:184-189). The actual default values depend on the installed SI version at insert_default() time.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Kilosort4Schema (v2/_params/sorter.py:91-112) curates explicit defaults Th_universal=9.0, Th_learned=8.0, nblocks=1, max_cluster_subset=25_000, do_CAR=True with extra='allow' for SI's other kwargs. Decoupled from sis.get_default_sorter_params().
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2's curated KS4 defaults pin specific knob values; v1's defaults track whatever SI shipped. If SI's defaults shift, v2 is stable while v1 silently shifts. v2 max_cluster_subset=25_000 specifically is a v2 design choice. extra='allow' preserves v1's escape hatch for other KS4 kwargs.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented at _params/sorter.py:94-104 — v2 curates the most-used knobs; allows escape hatch. KS4 non-determinism is also called out (CPU/GPU runtime differences).

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed both code references directly:

v1/sorting.py:184-189 reads:
```
contents.extend(
    [
        [sorter, "default", sis.get_default_sorter_params(sorter)]
        for sorter in sis.available_sorters()
    ]
)
```
So v1 dynamically loads SI's defaults for every installed sorter (including KS4) at insert_default() time — defaults silently track whatever SI version is installed.

v2/_params/sorter.py:91-112 reads:
```
class Kilosort4Schema(BaseModel):
    ...
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")
    schema_version: int = 1
    Th_universal: float = Field(default=9.0, gt=0.0)
    Th_learned: float = Field(default=8.0, gt=0.0)
    nblocks: int = Field(default=1, ge=0)
    max_cluster_subset: int = Field(default=25_000, ge=1)
    do_CAR: bool = True
```
The curated defaults are exactly as the finding claims, and extra='allow' preserves v1's escape hatch for other KS4 kwargs.

The docstring at lines 94-104 explicitly documents the design choice: "types the most-used knobs but uses extra='allow' so users who need an undocumented KS4 kwarg ... can pass it through to SI", "Matches v1's escape hatch at v1/sorting.py:184-189", and "KS4 is not a deterministic fallback (CPU/GPU runtime differences can change spike times) and may require GPU".

v2 additionally has GenericSorterParamsSchema (line 186-196) as a permissive fallback for any other installed SI sorter, and _SORTER_SCHEMAS registry (line 199-206) routes by sorter name. This preserves v1's "try any installed SI sorter" while gating the most-used sorters behind typed schemas.

The divergence is real (v1 tracks SI's defaults, v2 pins specific values like max_cluster_subset=25_000), inline-documented, and consistent with v2's stated schema-first / correctness-first design goal. Medium severity is appropriate — defaults can affect sort outputs but extra='allow' preserves user override paths, and v2's stability against SI default drift is the intended improvement.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 Recording does not register sort_valid_times to IntervalList (v1 always did)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: SpikeSortingRecording.make_insert writes a new IntervalList row with interval_list_name=key['recording_id'], pipeline='spikesorting_recording_v1', valid_times=sort_interval_valid_times. Downstream callers can then look up the sort interval by recording_id (v1/utils.py:77 uses this convention).
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Recording.make_insert only writes the master row (with electrical_series_path, object_id, n_channels, etc.). No IntervalList row keyed by recording_id is ever created. The sort_valid_times array is fetched per-call from the upstream IntervalList row identified by RecordingSelection.interval_list_name, but no recording_id-scoped IntervalList row is materialized.
confirmed_findings.v2_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1077-1090 (insert1 only); grep IntervalList.insert in v2/recording.py returns nothing
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/recording.py has zero IntervalList.insert/insert1 calls inside Recording. Downstream code (v1/utils.py:77) constructs queries assuming a recording_id-named IntervalList exists; any external/notebook code that does `IntervalList & {'nwb_file_name': ..., 'interval_list_name': recording_id}` against v2-produced rows will fetch zero rows. There is no docstring or shared-contracts.md note explaining why this side-effect was dropped.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: no documented rationale

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the core behavioral divergence: v1 writes a recording_id-keyed IntervalList row, v2 does not.

v1 evidence — src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:211-217 sets the IntervalList key with `interval_list_name=key["recording_id"]` and `pipeline="spikesorting_recording_v1"`; lines 255-257 insert it:
```
IntervalList.insert1(
    sort_interval_valid_times.as_dict, skip_duplicates=True
)
```

v2 evidence — src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1077-1090 only inserts the master Recording row (electrical_series_path, object_id, n_channels, sampling_frequency, duration_s, cache_hash, timestamps_adjusted, n_adjusted_samples). A repo-wide grep `IntervalList.insert` against v2 returns exactly one hit (v2/artifact.py:825 for artifact intervals), confirming Recording never materializes the sort_valid_times row. v2/recording.py:854-860 instead fetches sort_valid_times from the upstream RecordingSelection.interval_list_name on every call.

No rationale documented: shared-contracts.md (.claude/docs/plans/spikesorting-v2/shared-contracts.md) discusses IntervalList only for artifact rows; the Recording class docstring at v2/recording.py:780-792 does not explain dropping the row.

One refinement to the finding's claim: the cited downstream consumer at v1/utils.py:77 does NOT actually look up IntervalList by recording_id. That line is `rec_dict = {"recording_id": str(r_id), "interval_list_name": str(a_id)}` where `a_id` is the artifact_id, and the query is against SpikeSortingSelection (line 79), not IntervalList. v1/sorting.py:259-265 likewise looks up IntervalList by the SpikeSortingSelection.interval_list_name FK (artifact_id or upstream interval), not recording_id. v1/artifact.py:169-172 also uses the upstream interval_list_name. So no production code path in v1/ actually consumes the recording_id-keyed IntervalList row — it functions as a discovery / introspection artifact (e.g., notebook 02_Insert_Data.ipynb and 41_Decoding_Clusterless.ipynb display IntervalList rows with `pipeline='spikesorting_recording_v1'`).

Net result: the divergence is real (v1 writes, v2 does not; no documented rationale) but the consumer cited in the finding is misidentified, so the practical risk is limited to external/notebook users browsing IntervalList by recording_id or by `pipeline='spikesorting_recording_v1'`. No framework code paths break. Classification remains intentional_unjustified, but severity is overstated — medium is more appropriate than high given no production read site is impacted.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 artifact IntervalList uses 'artifact_{uuid}' prefix instead of v1's bare str(uuid)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: ArtifactDetection.make inserts IntervalList row with interval_list_name=str(key['artifact_id']) (the bare UUID string) and pipeline='spikesorting_artifact_v1'.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: ArtifactDetection.make_insert writes interval_list_name=artifact_interval_list_name(artifact_id) which returns f'artifact_{artifact_id}' (prefixed) and pipeline='spikesorting_artifact_v2'.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/utils.py:513-519 docstring explicitly centralizes the 'artifact_' prefix and ships a `parse_artifact_interval_list_name` inverse. The prefix makes the IntervalList rows greppable / distinguishable from non-artifact intervals, which is a stated improvement. But it does break any v1 downstream code that fetched IntervalList by bare artifact_id UUID.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: documented in v2/utils.py:512-519 ('Centralizes the v2 convention'); merge-dispatcher contract uses parse_artifact_interval_list_name as inverse

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all three claims by reading the code directly.

(1) v1 artifact.py:194-205 inserts the IntervalList row with `interval_list_name=str(key["artifact_id"])` (line 200, bare UUID string) and `pipeline="spikesorting_artifact_v1"` (line 202).

(2) v2 artifact.py:807 computes `interval_list_name = artifact_interval_list_name(key["artifact_id"])`, and the insert rows on lines 813-821 set `pipeline="spikesorting_artifact_v2"` (line 818). The v2 version also writes one row per member nwb_file_name (multi-row) rather than v1's single row.

(3) v2 utils.py:509-519 defines `_ARTIFACT_INTERVAL_LIST_PREFIX = "artifact_"` and `artifact_interval_list_name` returns `f"{_ARTIFACT_INTERVAL_LIST_PREFIX}{artifact_id}"`. Docstring at lines 513-518 explicitly says "Centralizes the v2 convention `f\"artifact_{artifact_id}\"` so the prefix lives in one place; `parse_artifact_interval_list_name` is its inverse."

(4) The inverse parser at utils.py:522-533 confirms the round-trip contract and references the merge-dispatcher's "leave non-artifact names alone" contract.

The classification (intentional_justified) is supported: the prefix is documented in code, has a centralized helper plus an inverse parser, and the v2 pipeline tag also changed in lockstep. The medium severity is appropriate: any v1-era downstream code that fetched IntervalList by bare UUID would silently miss v2 artifact rows.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 ElectricalSeries 'filtering' / 'description' attribute strings differ from v1
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: filtering='Bandpass filtered for spike band'; description=f'Referenced and filtered recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting'; table_region description='Sort group'.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: filtering='Bandpass filter + common reference'; description=f'Pre-motion preprocessed recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting'; table_region description='Sort group electrodes'.
confirmed_findings.evidence: The strings are surface-level metadata only and no consumer code in the repository keys off them. But v2 changed both `filtering` (which now claims 'common reference' is part of the filter chain) and `description` (now says 'Pre-motion preprocessed') without any code comment justifying the wording change. NWB-Inspector or downstream DANDI publication tooling that compares filtering strings across files would see a non-parity diff.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: no documented rationale; 'Pre-motion' likely reflects the SortingAnalyzer pipeline where motion correction is deferred to sorter stage

verdict.reasoning:
Citations verified exactly as stated.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:840-857):
- table_region description="Sort group"
- filtering="Bandpass filtered for spike band"
- description="Referenced and filtered recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting"

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1895-1909):
- table_region description="Sort group electrodes"
- filtering="Bandpass filter + common reference"
- description="Pre-motion preprocessed recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting"

Grep confirms these strings are SET but never READ by any code in the repo. No consumer keys off them.

However, the rationale that the finding labels as "missing" is actually documented at the module level. src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:12-14 module docstring states: "The recording write applies bandpass filtering + common-reference referencing (no whitening; that is deferred to the sort stage so motion correction never sees whitened data)." And `_apply_pre_motion_preprocessing` at line 1725-1735 documents the deferred-whitening architecture: "Whitening is deliberately deferred to the sorter stage so motion correction never sees whitened data (SpikeInterface docs flag whitening as destructive for motion estimators)."

So:
- The wording change is technically correct: v1's "Bandpass filtered for spike band" did not mention referencing even though v1 also applied referencing; v2's "Bandpass filter + common reference" is a more faithful description of what's actually in the saved ElectricalSeries.
- "Pre-motion preprocessed" reflects the documented v2 architecture (whitening deferred so motion correction sees clean signal).
- The rationale exists in the module docstring and the preprocessing helper docstring, just not inline at the ElectricalSeries call site.

Classification holds as intentional, with documented justification (module docstring + helper docstring). The drift in surface metadata is real and would show up in NWB-Inspector diffs, but severity is genuinely low because no code consumes the strings and the new wording is more accurate.

The finding's "unjustified" claim is slightly overstated — there IS module-level documentation of why the preprocessing chain changed, which justifies the new wording. But re-classifying as "intentional_justified" with low severity is the more accurate verdict.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 sorting writes obs_intervals fallback to full timestamps envelope when artifact_id is None; v1 always passes artifact-removed intervals
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _write_sorting_to_nwb always receives `sort_interval` = artifact_removed_intervals from the artifact IntervalList; if 1D it is reshaped to (1,2); nwbf.add_unit(obs_intervals=obs_interval, ...) for every unit.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _write_units_nwb receives obs_intervals=None when sel_row['artifact_id'] is None (line 514) and falls back to obs_intervals_arr = _np.asarray([[float(timestamps[0]), float(timestamps[-1])]]) -- the full recording envelope. The fallback envelope is wider than the artifact-removed intervals, so downstream firing-rate calcs over an unmasked sort report observation across the whole recording.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/sorting.py:1467-1469 explicitly documents 'obs_intervals defaults to the recording's full timestamp envelope when no artifact mask was applied'. v2 SortingSelection makes ArtifactDetection FK nullable (v1 required an artifact selection), so the no-artifact case is a new shape that needs a fallback. The fallback is internally consistent with 'no artifact mask was applied'.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: documented in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py:1462-1469 ('no-mask sort semantics (the sort observed every sample)')

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all cited code locations.

v1 evidence (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/sorting.py):
- Lines 198-206: SpikeSortingSelection has a required `-> IntervalList` FK -- non-nullable.
- Lines 259-265: `make_fetch` always fetches `artifact_removed_intervals` from the IntervalList associated with `recording_key["interval_list_name"]`.
- Lines 587-598: `_write_sorting_to_nwb` reshapes `sort_interval` to (1,2) if 1D and passes `obs_intervals=obs_interval` for every unit. No None code path exists.

v2 evidence (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/sorting.py):
- Lines 215-220: `SortingSelection` definition has `-> [nullable] ArtifactDetection` -- nullable FK, a deliberate schema change from v1.
- Lines 489-514: `make_fetch` returns `obs_intervals=None` when `sel_row.get("artifact_id") is None`; otherwise fetches valid_times from the artifact IntervalList.
- Lines 1462-1469: Docstring explicitly states 'obs_intervals defaults to the recording's full timestamp envelope when no artifact mask was applied (obs_intervals=None)'.
- Lines 1480-1486: Implements the fallback: `obs_intervals_arr = _np.asarray([[float(timestamps[0]), float(timestamps[-1])]])` -- the full timestamps envelope.
- Lines 1481-1483 comment: 'Fall back to the full recording window when no artifact IntervalList was supplied; matches the no-mask sort semantics (the sort observed every sample).'

The finding's claim is structurally accurate: v2 introduces a new None pathway and a wider-than-v1 obs_intervals fallback, gated by the new nullable schema. The behavior is correct for the new semantics (no artifact mask -> sort observed the whole recording), is explicitly documented, and the severity-low classification is appropriate because (a) the wider envelope only manifests when the user opts into the nullable artifact_id, and (b) reporting the full envelope is the semantically correct answer for an unmasked sort. Intentional_justified holds.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 curation no longer writes the 'merge_groups' NWB column; provenance lives only in DataJoint MergeGroup part
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _write_sorting_to_nwb_with_curation calls nwbf.add_unit_column(name='merge_groups', description='merge groups', data=merge_groups_list, index=True) -- a per-unit ragged column on the curated units NWB. External readers (v1/figurl_curation.py reads via nwb_sorting.get('merge_groups')) can recover the merge dictionary from the NWB alone.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: CurationV2._write_curated_nwb adds only the 'curation_label' column (when non-empty). The MergeGroup part table on CurationV2 stores merge provenance as (kept_unit_id, contributor_unit_id) pairs but NOTHING about merges is written into the AnalysisNwbfile. A consumer that opens the curated NWB without a DB connection cannot reconstruct merge groups.
confirmed_findings.v2_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:101-130 (MergeGroup part definition); v2/curation.py:864-876 (only curation_label written, no merge_groups branch); v2/curation.py:104-110 docstring 'replaced v1's merge_groups NWB column with a per-unit kept-set'
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/curation.py:104-110 explicitly documents the change: 'Restores v1's merge-group recoverability lost when v2 replaced v1's merge_groups NWB column with a per-unit kept-set'. The MergeGroup part table is documented as the queryable replacement. But this is a NWB-on-disk parity break: any code that reads merged units from the NWB without DataJoint (DANDI publication, kachery shares, external collaborators) loses merge information. v1/figurl_curation.py:83-101 pattern (which v2's curation comment quotes) would silently see no merge information.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: documented in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:101-130 and 104-110; rationale is 'queryable provenance' over file-level provenance

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against code.

v1 writes merge_groups as a per-unit ragged NWB column (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:404-415):
```
if merge_groups is not None:
    merge_groups_dict = _list_to_merge_dict(merge_groups, unit_ids)
    merge_groups_list = [[""] if value == [] else value for value in merge_groups_dict.values()]
    nwbf.add_unit_column(name="merge_groups", description="merge groups", data=merge_groups_list, index=True)
```

v1 reads it back from the NWB alone (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/figurl_curation.py:85):
```
merge_groups = list(nwb_sorting.get("merge_groups", []))
```

v2 _write_curated_nwb (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:821-888) has exactly ONE add_unit_column call, and it writes curation_label only (lines 864-876). Confirmed via `grep add_unit_column src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/`: only line 865 in curation.py (curation_label) and lines 1505/1510 in sorting.py (sorting-side columns, not curation). NO merge_groups column is ever written.

The MergeGroup part table at v2/curation.py:101-130 explicitly documents the swap in its docstring: "Restores v1's merge-group recoverability lost when v2 replaced v1's merge_groups NWB column with a per-unit kept-set... v2 stores them here as queryable part rows so bulk-audit queries... and provenance retrieval... are easy."

Classification holds: intentional_justified. The change is documented with a stated rationale (queryable provenance over file-level provenance) and acknowledged as a deliberate replacement of v1's NWB column. But it IS a real NWB-on-disk parity break: any consumer reading the curated NWB without a DataJoint connection (DANDI publication, kachery shares, external collaborators using pynwb directly) cannot reconstruct merge groups. v1's own figurl_curation pattern (v1/figurl_curation.py:85) would silently see no merges if pointed at a v2-written NWB -- nwb_sorting.get("merge_groups", []) returns the default [].

Severity high is defensible: not a numeric/correctness regression, but a permanent loss of merge provenance for any off-database consumer. Caveat: v2/figurl_curation.py and v2/figpack_curation.py are stub files per repo context, so in-repo callers aren't yet exposed -- the risk is concentrated in external NWB readers and future ports of those stubs.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 Recording does not persist 'electrodes_id' (object_id of the electrodes sub-object); v1 stores both
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: SpikeSortingRecording definition stores three identity fields: object_id (varchar(40)) for the ProcessedElectricalSeries, electrodes_id (varchar(40)) for ProcessedElectricalSeries/electrodes, hash (varchar(32)). _write_recording_to_nwb captures electrodes_id = nwbfile.acquisition[series_name].electrodes.object_id and the recompute machinery (v1/recompute.py + RecordingRecompute) uses electrodes_id to verify the electrodes sub-object matches across rebuilds.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Recording definition stores object_id (varchar(72)) plus electrical_series_path (varchar(255)), n_channels, sampling_frequency, duration_s, cache_hash (char(64)), timestamps_adjusted, n_adjusted_samples. There is no electrodes_id column and _write_nwb_artifact does not capture or persist nwbfile.acquisition[...].electrodes.object_id.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 has no recompute machinery (RecordingRecompute/Versions/Selection are listed as v1 features absent in v2). cache_hash via NwbfileHasher hashes the whole file, which subsumes the electrodes sub-object. The trade is that an external verifier wanting to compare just the electrodes object_id (without re-hashing the file) cannot.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: implicit: v2 design drops the recompute chain and uses whole-file NwbfileHasher (see v2/recording.py:1797-1803 'shared-contracts.md Recording Cache Format. The v1 recompute machinery uses the same hashing path, so v2 verification does not maintain a parallel implementation.')

verdict.reasoning:
Verified all claims against source.

v1 schema at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:187-195:
```
definition = """
# Processed recording.
-> SpikeSortingRecordingSelection
---
-> AnalysisNwbfile
object_id: varchar(40) # Object ID for the processed recording in NWB file
electrodes_id=null: varchar(40) # Object ID for the processed electrodes
hash=null: varchar(32) # Hash of the NWB file
"""
```

v1 capture at v1/recording.py:863-864:
```
recording_object_id = nwbfile.acquisition[series_name].object_id
electrodes_id = nwbfile.acquisition[series_name].electrodes.object_id
```

v2 schema at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:794-806:
```
definition = """
-> RecordingSelection
---
-> AnalysisNwbfile
electrical_series_path: varchar(255)
object_id: varchar(72)
n_channels: int
sampling_frequency: float
duration_s: float
cache_hash: char(64)
timestamps_adjusted=0: bool
n_adjusted_samples=0: int
"""
```
No `electrodes_id` column.

v2 write at v2/recording.py:1912-1915 only captures `object_id = nwbfile.acquisition[_ELECTRICAL_SERIES_NAME].object_id`; no electrodes sub-object id.

Grep confirms zero occurrences of `electrodes_id` or `electrodes.object_id` anywhere in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/, and zero occurrences of `RecordingRecompute` in v2.

v2 instead uses `_hash_nwb_recording` / `NwbfileHasher` whole-file hashing. The inline comment at v2/recording.py:1917-1923 explicitly states the design intent: "Hash the persisted file (not in-memory bytes) so the digest reflects what was actually written -- timestamps, electrodes, conversion, ElectricalSeries metadata -- not just trace data. Matches shared-contracts.md Recording Cache Format and the v1 recompute hashing path."

The classification as intentional_justified is correct: the recompute family (RecordingRecompute/Versions/Selection) was deliberately not ported, replaced by a single `cache_hash` that subsumes electrodes identity along with all other persisted bytes. Severity medium is appropriate — an external verifier that wants to compare just the electrodes object_id without re-hashing the whole file loses that ability, but the whole-file hash strictly dominates the per-object check for correctness purposes.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 Sorting stores time_of_sort as datetime; v1 stores as Unix int seconds
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: SpikeSorting definition: `time_of_sort: int  # in Unix time, to the nearest second`. make_compute sets `time_of_sort = int(time.time())`.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Sorting definition: `time_of_sort: datetime`. make_insert sets `time_of_sort = _dt.datetime.now()`.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Schema-level type change (int -> datetime) without a code comment justifying the migration. datetime is arguably cleaner than Unix-seconds but it breaks any v1 caller that did `time.gmtime(row['time_of_sort'])`. There is also no timezone awareness on `datetime.now()` (naive datetime) which is a future correctness risk.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: no documented rationale

verdict.reasoning: Directly verified all cited locations. v1/sorting.py:239 declares `time_of_sort: int               # in Unix time, to the nearest second`; v1/sorting.py:295 sets `time_of_sort = int(time.time())`. v2/sorting.py:429 declares `time_of_sort: datetime`; v2/sorting.py:676 sets `"time_of_sort": _dt.datetime.now()` with `import datetime as _dt` at line 660. The `datetime.now()` call is naive (no tz=), confirming the timezone-awareness risk. v0 also uses int Unix time (v0/spikesorting_sorting.py:181), so v2 is the lone outlier. No comment, docstring, or commit message explains the migration at either line 429 or 676 — confirmed via Read of surrounding context. No v2 test in tests/spikesorting/v2/ references `time_of_sort` (only legacy_schemas.json baselines record v0/v1's int form), so the change is untested. tests/conftest.py:1844 orders by `time_of_sort desc`, which works at the SQL level for both types, but any downstream Python caller treating the field as Unix seconds (e.g., `time.gmtime(row['time_of_sort'])`) would break. Severity is appropriately low since the field is metadata-only and not used in computation.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 ElectricalSeries write raises on heterogeneous channel gains; v1 silently picks gains[0]
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: _write_recording_to_nwb passes `conversion=np.unique(recording.get_channel_gains())[0] * 1e-6` -- picks the first unique gain even when channels have heterogeneous gains. The resulting ElectricalSeries.conversion is applied uniformly across channels, silently mis-scaling channels whose gains differ from the chosen one.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _write_nwb_artifact computes `gains = _np.unique(recording.get_channel_gains())`; if `len(gains) != 1` it raises ValueError('Recording.make: recording has heterogeneous channel gains ...'). Otherwise `conversion = float(gains[0]) * 1e-6`.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/recording.py:1853-1861 explicitly calls out v1's behavior as 'a latent correctness bug: the chosen gain becomes the universal conversion factor on the ElectricalSeries, so signals on channels with heterogeneous gains are scaled by the wrong factor.' v2 catches at populate time rather than producing wrongly-scaled NWBs. This is a correctness improvement over v1, NOT a regression -- noting because the task asks for behavioral divergences.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: documented inline at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1853-1869 and referenced to shared-contracts.md

verdict.reasoning:
Verified both code locations directly.

v1 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:858 confirms:
`conversion=np.unique(recording.get_channel_gains())[0] * 1e-6,`
This silently picks the first unique gain. If channels have heterogeneous gains, np.unique returns multiple values and [0] selects only one, which becomes the universal conversion factor for the ElectricalSeries -- silently mis-scaling other channels.

v2 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1862-1870 confirms:
```
gains = _np.unique(recording.get_channel_gains())
if len(gains) != 1:
    raise ValueError(
        "Recording.make: recording has heterogeneous channel "
        f"gains {gains.tolist()}; v2 ElectricalSeries write "
        "requires a single conversion factor. Verify probe "
        "metadata for the sort group."
    )
conversion = float(gains[0]) * 1e-6
```

The v2 code is preceded by an explicit comment block (lines 1853-1861) that documents v1's behavior as "a latent correctness bug" and justifies catching it at populate time rather than producing wrongly-scaled NWBs, with a reference to shared-contracts.md.

This is a documented, intentional correctness improvement over v1. The classification 'intentional_justified' and severity 'high' are appropriate -- the latent v1 bug could silently mis-scale ephys data on probes with mixed gains, which would propagate into all downstream analyses. v2 fails loudly at the input-validation boundary, consistent with the schema-first / defense-in-depth identity noted in repo context.

Note: the finding describes a v2 *behavioral* divergence (raises vs silently picks), which is what the task asks for. It is not a v2 regression; it is v2 surfacing a v1 bug.

confirmed_findings.title: v1 hardcodes 30 kHz in TimestampsExtractor; v2 propagates the actual recording sampling_frequency
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: TimestampsExtractor.__init__ defaults `sampling_frequency=30e3`. _write_recording_to_nwb constructs `TimestampsDataChunkIterator(recording=TimestampsExtractor(timestamps), buffer_gb=5)` -- using the default 30 kHz regardless of recording's actual sampling rate.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: _TimestampsExtractor still defaults 30e3, but TimestampsDataChunkIterator takes a `sampling_frequency` param and the v2 writer passes `sampling_frequency = float(recording.get_sampling_frequency())` (the real rate).
confirmed_findings.v1_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:973 (default 30e3); v1/recording.py:847-849 (no sampling_frequency override at call site)
confirmed_findings.v2_location: src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_nwb_iterators.py:127 (default 30e3 retained in private extractor); src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1878 (caller resolves true rate); v2/recording.py:1885-1889 (passes sampling_frequency)
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2/_nwb_iterators.py:17-24 explicitly redesigns the API: 'v1 wraps a 1D timestamps vector in a BaseRecording subclass (TimestampsExtractor) purely to satisfy its TimestampsDataChunkIterator constructor signature ... v2 collapses that indirection by accepting timestamps and sampling_frequency directly.' The hardcoded 30 kHz in v1 likely never affected actual data correctness because the iterator only iterates the 1D timestamps array (chunk shape doesn't depend on the value), but it was a latent bug if a non-30 kHz recording were ever encountered. v2 fixes it without breaking parity.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: documented in src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/_nwb_iterators.py:17-24

verdict.reasoning:
Verified by direct read of all four cited locations.

(1) v1/recording.py:969-984 defines `TimestampsExtractor.__init__(self, timestamps, sampling_frequency=30e3)` — default 30e3 confirmed at line 973.

(2) v1/recording.py:847-848 calls `TimestampsDataChunkIterator(recording=TimestampsExtractor(timestamps), buffer_gb=5)` — no `sampling_frequency` override at call site, so the 30e3 default is always used in v1 regardless of `recording.get_sampling_frequency()`.

(3) v2/_nwb_iterators.py:124-138 keeps a private `_TimestampsExtractor` with the same `sampling_frequency=30e3` default — confirmed at line 127.

(4) v2/_nwb_iterators.py:141-173 — the public `TimestampsDataChunkIterator` now takes `timestamps: np.ndarray` and `sampling_frequency: float` as positional parameters (lines 153-154, no default on sampling_frequency). It instantiates `_TimestampsExtractor(timestamps=timestamps, sampling_frequency=sampling_frequency)` (lines 162-165), so the private extractor's 30e3 default is unreachable from the public API.

(5) v2/recording.py:1878-1889 — the writer resolves `sampling_frequency = float(recording.get_sampling_frequency())` (line 1878) and passes it explicitly (lines 1885-1888).

(6) Documentation: v2/_nwb_iterators.py:17-24 (module docstring) explicitly describes the redesign — "v1 wraps a 1D timestamps vector in a BaseRecording subclass (TimestampsExtractor) purely to satisfy its TimestampsDataChunkIterator constructor signature ... v2 collapses that indirection by accepting timestamps and sampling_frequency directly." This is an intentional, documented API change.

(7) Correctness impact: the iterator's `_get_maxshape` (v2/_nwb_iterators.py:192-193) returns `(n_samples,)` from the timestamps array length, and `get_traces` (lines 115-121 / v1 lines 998-1005) slices the raw `_timeseries` array. The wrapper's `sampling_frequency` only sets BaseRecording metadata; it does not affect the bytes written to the ElectricalSeries `timestamps` dataset. So the v1 hardcoded 30 kHz was a latent bug (would have surfaced if SpikeInterface internals ever consulted the wrapper's sampling rate for chunking), not active data corruption. v2's fix is a defensive cleanup that also documents intent.

All four file:line claims hold. Classification intentional_justified, severity low — correct.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 ElectricalSeries write tests assert specific shape, but parity tests (test_v1_parity.py) do not verify ProcessedElectricalSeries metadata vs v1
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1 NWB writes filtering / description / conversion / electrodes_id / object_id; no test in v1 enforces the exact string values of filtering or description.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: test_single_session_pipeline.py line 2865-2879 constructs a ProcessedElectricalSeries with `filtering='bandpass'` and `conversion=1e-6` -- but this is for a regression fixture, not a parity check. test_v1_parity.py (896 lines) only references 'get_merge_groups' once and has zero assertions about ElectricalSeries filtering/description/conversion/object_id parity with v1's strings.
confirmed_findings.v1_location: tests/spikesorting/v1/ (1.1k lines, light); no ElectricalSeries metadata assertions
confirmed_findings.evidence: Grep over tests/spikesorting/v2/ for ProcessedElectricalSeries, buffer_gb, conversion, filtering, description=, merge_groups, add_unit_column, obs_intervals returns mostly fixture-construction lines, not assertions that v2 outputs match v1 metadata. The intentional changes to filtering string ('Bandpass filter + common reference') and description ('Pre-motion preprocessed recording') would not be caught by a parity test if v1 strings were changed back.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: no documented rationale

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the finding by reading code directly.

1) The v2 NWB-write metadata for ProcessedElectricalSeries diverges from v1:
   - v1 src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py:855 sets `filtering="Bandpass filtered for spike band"`; v2 src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py:1904 sets `filtering="Bandpass filter + common reference"`.
   - v1 recording.py:856-857 sets description=`"Referenced and filtered recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting"`; v2 recording.py:1905-1908 sets description=`"Pre-motion preprocessed recording from {nwb_file_name} for spike sorting"`.
   - v1 recording.py:842 sets electrode table region `description="Sort group"`; v2 recording.py:1897 sets `description="Sort group electrodes"`.
   - v1 recording.py:858 sets `conversion=np.unique(recording.get_channel_gains())[0] * 1e-6`; v2 recording.py:1909 passes a `conversion` variable from upstream — different derivation path.

2) tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py (896 lines) has zero references to `ProcessedElectricalSeries`, `filtering`, `Bandpass`, `Pre-motion`, `Referenced`, `Sort group`, or `conversion`. The only `merge_groups`-family reference is at line 521 (`"get_merge_groups"`), which is part of a `@classmethod` decorator check (lines 516-527), not a behavioral parity assertion about the strings or contents.

3) tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py:2870-2879 constructs an ElectricalSeries with `filtering='bandpass'` and `conversion=1e-6` purely as a synthetic fixture for `_recording_t_start` regression (test_apply...t_start). It is NOT a parity check against v1 strings — the strings used ('bandpass', 'synthetic for t_start regression') don't match v1 ('Bandpass filtered for spike band') OR v2 ('Bandpass filter + common reference') production values.

4) tests/spikesorting/v2/_parity_canonical.py and test_parity_canonical.py cover only param canonicalization (sorter / preproc / artifact dicts) — no NWB-output metadata comparisons.

5) `grep -rn "assert.*filtering|assert.*Bandpass|assert.*Pre-motion|assert.*Referenced|assert.*ProcessedElectricalSeries"` across tests/spikesorting/ returns zero hits. No test in v0, v1, or v2 asserts these specific NWB metadata strings.

Conclusion: the branch is genuinely untested. If a future refactor changed the v2 strings back to v1 form (or to anything else), no test would catch it. The metadata-string divergence itself reads as a likely intentional rewording (v2's "Bandpass filter + common reference" and "Pre-motion preprocessed" accurately describe v2's pre-motion-correction processing stage) — but neither v1 nor v2 ever documents the intent, and no parity test pins the contract either way.

Severity is medium: ElectricalSeries metadata strings are user-facing in NWB files / DANDI exports and are part of the documented dataset, but a string change does not corrupt numerical results. The conversion-derivation divergence (v1 reads from channel gains; v2 takes a variable from caller) is more substantive but is also outside the scope of this string-focused finding.

Relevant absolute paths:
- /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/recording.py (lines 840-863)
- /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/recording.py (lines 1895-1914)
- /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py (line 521 — only merge reference; nothing on ElectricalSeries metadata)
- /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/tests/spikesorting/v2/test_single_session_pipeline.py (lines 2870-2879 — synthetic fixture, not parity)
- /cumulus/edeno/spyglass/tests/spikesorting/v2/_parity_canonical.py and test_parity_canonical.py (params-only canonicalization, no NWB metadata)

confirmed_findings.title: CurationV2.get_sort_group_info returns ALL electrodes per sort group; v1 returns one row per group (limit=1)
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: CurationV1.get_sort_group_info builds electrode_restrict_list by iterating sort groups and doing `(SortGroup.SortGroupElectrode & entry * Electrode).fetch(limit=1)` -- one electrode per group -- then joins to BrainRegion and returns a dj.Table with one (group, region) row.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: CurationV2.get_sort_group_info returns the relation `(SortGroupV2.SortGroupElectrode & sg_restriction) * Electrode * BrainRegion` -- one row per electrode, not per group. Multi-electrode probes produce multiple rows.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at curation.py:1195-1207 explicitly says "Fix for the v1 fetch(limit=1) multi-region under-reporting bug ... returns a DataJoint relation covering EVERY electrode in the sort group so a multi-region probe surfaces every represented region." The merge dispatcher at spikesorting_merge.py:412-413 does `part_table * sort_group_info` -- a v1 caller doing `.fetch1()` on the result would now raise on multi-electrode probes. Tests in tests/spikesorting/v2/test_downstream_consumers.py:189-203 and test_single_session_pipeline.py:5663-5700 assert the v2 shape but do not verify v1 callers still work.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Documented in v2/curation.py:1196-1201 as a deliberate fix for v1's multi-region under-reporting. The shape change is a breaking API change for any caller relying on single-row return; no migration shim provided.

verdict.reasoning:
All claims verified in code.

v1 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:288-295) explicitly iterates sort groups and does `((SortGroup.SortGroupElectrode() & entry) * Electrode).fetch(limit=1)` -- one row per group. The trailing `(cls & key).proj() * sort_group_info` returns that join.

v2 (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:1234-1236) returns `(SortGroupV2.SortGroupElectrode & sg_restriction) * _Electrode * BrainRegion` with NO limit -- one row per electrode. Docstring at line 1195-1207 explicitly says: "Fix for the v1 ``fetch(limit=1)`` multi-region under-reporting bug ... returns a DataJoint relation ... covering EVERY electrode in the sort group so a multi-region probe surfaces every represented region."

Merge dispatcher (src/spyglass/spikesorting/spikesorting_merge.py:412-413) does `sort_group_info = source_table.get_sort_group_info(query.fetch("KEY"))` then `return part_table * sort_group_info`. This propagates the multi-row v2 shape to callers.

Breaking-change risk is real: v1 test at tests/spikesorting/v1/test_merge.py:40 does `spike_merge.get_sort_group_info(pop_spike_merge).fetch1()` -- a `.fetch1()` call that would raise `DataJointError` on a multi-electrode v2 probe (tetrodes return 4 rows). No migration shim or compatibility flag is provided.

v2 tests assert the new shape only: test_downstream_consumers.py:189-203 checks `len(rows) > 0` and presence of `electrode_id`/`region_name`; test_single_session_pipeline.py:5663-5700 (`test_merge_dispatch_get_sort_group_info_works_for_v2`) verifies non-empty rows but does NOT verify that v1 callers using `.fetch1()` still work. test_v1_parity.py:519 only checks classmethod-ness, not return shape.

Classification holds as intentional_justified: deliberate bug fix, documented in docstring, with rationale (v1 under-reported brain regions on multi-region probes). Severity high is appropriate because the merge-dispatcher path is shared API and `.fetch1()` callers will silently break with a runtime error on multi-electrode probes.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 CurationV2 omits NWB `curation_label` column when all labels are empty; v1 always writes column when labels arg is not None
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: `_write_sorting_to_nwb_with_curation` at v1/curation.py:391-403 writes the `curation_label` indexed column whenever `labels is not None` -- even if every per-unit label list is empty. With `labels={}` v1 writes one empty list per unit.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: `_stage_curated_units_nwb` at v2/curation.py:857-876 only adds the `curation_label` column when `any(all_labels)` is true. With `labels={}` or all-empty-lists v2 writes NO column.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 comment at curation.py:858-863 explains pynwb's dtype inference fails on an all-empty list-of-lists ("Cannot infer dtype of empty list"). Downstream readers at analysis/v1/group.py:210-217 handle missing-column via generator + .get(None, None) -> None, so v2 + empty labels still produces the unfiltered code path. The divergence: a v1 fetch_nwb consumer that does `nwb_file['object_id']['curation_label']` directly (without the SortedSpikesGroup helper) will KeyError on v2 empty-label rows where v1 returned a column of empty lists.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: pynwb dtype-inference limitation documented in source. The shape divergence on edge cases is not documented as a contract for downstream consumers; the test corpus does not include a v1-parity test that constructs the same labels arg on both pipelines and compares the resulting NWB column presence.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against source.

v1 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:391-403 unconditionally writes the `curation_label` indexed column whenever `labels is not None`. The inner loop appends `[]` for unit_ids missing from the dict, so `labels={}` produces a column of empty lists, one per unit:
```
if labels is not None:
    label_values = []
    for unit_id in unit_ids:
        if unit_id not in labels:
            label_values.append([])
        else:
            label_values.append(labels[unit_id])
    nwbf.add_unit_column(name="curation_label", ..., data=label_values, index=True)
```

v2 at src/spyglass/spikesorting/v2/curation.py:841-876 builds `all_labels` from `labels.get(int(unit_id), [])` (so `labels={}` yields all-empty lists), then gates the column on `any(all_labels)`:
```
if any(all_labels):
    nwbf.add_unit_column(name="curation_label", ..., data=all_labels, index=True)
```
With `labels={}` or any all-empty-lists case, v2 writes NO column. The intent is documented in-source at curation.py:857-863 and 776-785 explaining pynwb's "Cannot infer dtype of empty list or tuple" failure on an all-empty list-of-lists.

Downstream-reader claim also confirmed: src/spyglass/spikesorting/analysis/v1/group.py:210-217 uses a generator + `.get(next(group_col, None), None)` and gracefully treats missing column as "no filtering" — so the primary Spyglass consumer survives the divergence.

However, src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/figurl_curation.py:83-93 is a real v1-side consumer that directly checks `"curation_label" in nwb_sorting.columns` and raises ValueError if missing. While that consumer reads CurationV1 (not v2) outputs, it concretely demonstrates the "direct column access" pattern the finding flags.

The finding's central claim (v2 omits column when v1 would write a column of empty lists; documented pynwb-dtype reason; graceful primary reader; no parity test covering this edge) is correct on every cited line. Classification "intentional_justified" is supported by the in-source rationale; severity "medium" is defensible given (a) the undocumented external-consumer contract, (b) the lack of a `labels={}` v1-vs-v2 parity test, balanced against (c) the rarity of the `labels={}`/all-empty case in practice and (d) graceful handling by the principal downstream reader. One could argue "low" given the limited blast radius, but "medium" is reasonable.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 does not write the `merge_groups` NWB column; v1 stores merge groups as a units-table column
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: v1/curation.py:404-415 writes `merge_groups` as an `index=True` (ragged) column on the units table whenever `merge_groups is not None`. v1/curation.py:258-265 (`get_merged_sorting`) and v1/figurl_curation.py:85 read this column to recover the merge groups directly from the NWB.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: v2/curation.py:_stage_curated_units_nwb writes ONLY `spike_times` and (conditionally) `curation_label` columns. Merge groups live exclusively in the DataJoint `CurationV2.MergeGroup` part table; `get_merged_sorting` reads from the part table, never from NWB.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 part table docstring at curation.py:101-124 explicitly says: "Restores v1's merge-group recoverability lost when v2 replaced v1's merge_groups NWB column with a per-unit kept-set." Grep confirms only v0/v1 consumers (v1/figurl_curation.py:85, v1/metric_curation.py:483, v0/sortingview.py:59) read the `merge_groups` NWB column; the cross-pipeline consumers (analysis/v1/group.py, decoding/, unit_annotation.py) do not.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Schema rationale documented in curation.py:101-124 (queryable bulk-audit + provenance retrieval). Since no cross-pipeline consumer reads the NWB column, this is API-compatible; however, any user-script that hand-rolled `nwb_file['object_id'].get('merge_groups')` on v1 NWBs will silently see empty results on v2 NWBs.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified the finding by reading both implementations directly.

V1 WRITES merge_groups NWB column (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:404-415):
```
if merge_groups is not None:
    merge_groups_dict = _list_to_merge_dict(merge_groups, unit_ids)
    merge_groups_list = [...]
    nwbf.add_unit_column(
        name="merge_groups",
        description="merge groups",
        data=merge_groups_list,
        index=True,
    )
```

V1 READS it from NWB to reconstruct merges (src/spyglass/spikesorting/v1/curation.py:258-261):
```
merge_groups = nwb_sorting.get("merge_groups")
if merge_groups:
    units_to_merge = _merge_dict_to_list(merge_groups[:])
    return sc.MergeUnitsSorting(parent_sorting=si_sorting, units_to_merge=units_to_merge)
```

V2 does NOT write merge_groups NWB column. The only `add_unit_column` call in v2/curation.py is at line 865, and it is for `curation_label` (the only NWB column besides `spike_times`). `_stage_curated_units_nwb` writes only `spike_times` via `add_unit(...)` and conditionally `curation_label`.

V2's get_merged_sorting (v2/curation.py:1067-1109) reads the DataJoint part table via `cls.get_merge_groups(key)` — never opens the NWB to recover merges:
```
base = cls.get_sorting(key)
if bool((cls & key).fetch1("merges_applied")):
    return base
merge_groups = cls.get_merge_groups(key)
units_to_merge = [contribs for kept_uid, contribs in merge_groups.items() if len(contribs) > 1]
...
return sc.MergeUnitsSorting(base, units_to_merge=units_to_merge, delta_time_ms=None)
```

The v2 MergeGroup part-table docstring (curation.py:101-124) explicitly documents the rationale verbatim: "Restores v1's merge-group recoverability lost when v2 replaced v1's `merge_groups` NWB column with a per-unit kept-set. v1 stored merge groups as a single list-of-lists column on the units NWB; v2 stores them here as queryable part rows so bulk-audit queries [...] and provenance retrieval [...] are easy."

Grep across `src/spyglass/` for `merge_groups` found references only in spikesorting/{v0,v1,v2}/. The cross-pipeline consumers — analysis/v1/unit_annotation.py, analysis/v1/group.py, decoding/, common/, behavior/, lfp/, mua/, position/, ripple/ — have ZERO references. analysis/v1/unit_annotation.py reads only `spike_times` from NWB. So no in-repo cross-pipeline consumer breaks.

The only at-risk consumer is a user-script that hand-rolled `nwb['object_id'].get('merge_groups', [])` against v1 NWBs; that script will silently return empty on v2 NWBs because the column is absent (no KeyError, just empty). This silent-empty behavior is what justifies the medium severity rather than low.

All file:line citations in the original finding check out exactly.

confirmed_findings.title: test_get_restricted_merge_ids_default_sources_includes_v2 asserts wrong default; signature is sources=None not [v0, v1, v2]
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: N/A -- this is a v2-side test for the merge dispatcher API.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: Test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:374-380 asserts `sig.parameters['sources'].default == ['v0', 'v1', 'v2']`. But the actual signature at spikesorting_merge.py:301 is `sources: list | None = None`; the literal `['v0','v1','v2']` is built INSIDE the function via `_available_merge_sources()` (line 71-83) which conditionally appends 'v2'.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Direct read of signature default: line 301 `sources: list | None = None`. The test would fail under inspect.signature -- meaning either the test is currently broken/skipped or there is a mismatched contract between test intent and runtime behavior. The contract the docstring claims (lines 311-318) is that defaults are AVAILABLE sources, i.e. v0/v1 always, v2 only when imported. This contract is NOT what the test verifies.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: No documented rationale for the asymmetry. The test appears stale -- written against an earlier branch where the default was a literal list -- and was not updated when the lazy `_available_merge_sources()` indirection was added.

verdict.reasoning: Direct read confirms the finding. At src/spyglass/spikesorting/spikesorting_merge.py:301 the signature is `sources: list | None = None`. The literal `["v0", "v1", "v2"]` does not appear in the signature; it is built lazily inside the function via `_available_merge_sources()` at lines 71-83 (`sources = ["v0", "v1"]; if CurationV2 is not None: sources.append("v2"); return sources`), invoked at line 334 only when `sources is None`. The test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:374-380 asserts `sig.parameters["sources"].default == ["v0", "v1", "v2"]` — under inspect.signature this default is `None`, so the assertion is False and the test must fail against the current code. The docstring at lines 311-317 documents the contract as "every AVAILABLE source: ['v0','v1'] plus 'v2' only when the v2 module imported", which is a runtime/conditional contract that the test does not exercise. This matches the "Tests encode the contract" memory: the assertion does not enforce the docstring claim. The documented v2-conditional-inclusion branch has no regression guard. Production behavior is intentional and justified (avoids hard-failing v0/v1-only deployments when v2 import is absent), but the test is stale, written against an earlier literal-default shape, and was not updated when `_available_merge_sources()` indirection was introduced. Classification untested_branch and severity medium are both supported — no correctness regression in production code, but the documented contract has no test coverage and the existing test is broken.

confirmed_findings.title: v2 raises on unknown restriction keys; v1 silently drops them -- asymmetric semantics across mixed-source calls
confirmed_findings.v1_behavior: `_get_restricted_merge_ids_v1` at spikesorting_merge.py:250-296 silently ignores restriction keys that don't match any v1 attribute (DataJoint restriction with unknown attribute is a no-op on the projected table). A typo like `recording_id=...` against a v1 chain that doesn't carry recording_id would return the unrestricted v1 set.
confirmed_findings.v2_behavior: `_get_restricted_merge_ids_v2` at spikesorting_merge.py:190-197 explicitly checks `unknown = set(key) - allowed` and raises ValueError listing the offenders. The `allowed` set is the union of rec_keys, sort_keys, curation_keys.
confirmed_findings.evidence: v2 docstring at spikesorting_merge.py:153-156: "Unknown restriction keys raise ValueError rather than silently dropping through; v1 used to drop bad keys quietly, which made typos return wrong-but-non-empty results." The asymmetry means `get_restricted_merge_ids(key={...unknown_key...}, sources=['v1','v2'])` will RAISE in the v2 branch but succeed in v1 -- so the call cannot complete even if v1-only rows would have been returned. The test at tests/spikesorting/v2/test_v1_parity.py:364-371 covers this for the v2-only path but not the mixed-sources case.
confirmed_findings.rationale_if_known: Intentional v2 hardening documented in source. The asymmetry across sources is not explicitly addressed -- a mixed call short-circuits on v2's raise before v1 results can be returned, even when the user only cared about v1 rows.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified directly against source.
